**(a)** Explain with circuit diagram the **two-wattmeter method** for measurement of three-phase power. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two wattmeters connected to a three-phase supply read **5 kW** and **2 kW**. The phase sequence is RYB and the load is **balanced**. When the phase sequence is reversed, the readings become **2 kW** and **5 kW**. Find the power factor and the line current at **400 V** supply. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Circuit Connection:**
- Wattmeter $W_1$: Current coil in Line R, Pressure coil between R and B.
- Wattmeter $W_2$: Current coil in Line Y, Pressure coil between Y and B.
- Line B acts as common reference.

**Readings:**
$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ - \phi)$$

$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ + \phi)$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Three-wire system (no neutral current).
2. Supply voltages are sinusoidal and balanced.
3. Wattmeters are ideal (no coil resistance errors).
4. Phase sequence is positive (RYB) unless stated otherwise.

**Total Power:**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Observation:** Reversing phase sequence swaps the wattmeter readings ($W_1 \leftrightarrow W_2$). This confirms that the load is **balanced** (otherwise magnitudes would change upon reversal).

**Given:** With RYB → $W_1 = 5 \text{ kW}$, $W_2 = 2 \text{ kW}$ (both positive means $0.5 < \cos\phi \leq 1$).

**Step 1 — Phase Angle:**
$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}(5 - 2)}{5 + 2} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{7} \approx 0.7423$$

$$\phi \approx 36.59^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Power Factor:**
$$\cos\phi = \cos(36.59^\circ) \approx 0.803 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 3 — Line Current:**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 7 \text{ kW}$$

$$7000 = \sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot I_L \cdot 0.803$$

$$7000 = 556.3 \, I_L$$

$$I_L = 12.58 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using reverse sequence:**

With sequence reversed, formulas become $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ + \phi)$ and $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ - \phi)$.

So new $W_1 = 2 \text{ kW}$ (was previously $W_2$ formula), new $W_2 = 5 \text{ kW}$ (was previously $W_1$ formula). ✓ **Consistent with balanced load.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reverse $W_2$ sign** when reading is negative. If the meter needle deflects backward, **reverse the pressure coil connection** and record the reading as negative in the summation: $P = W_1 + (-W_2)$.
> 2. **Confusing phase sequence effect:** In RYB sequence with lagging PF, $W_1 > W_2$. In RBY sequence with the same load, $W_2 > W_1$. Don't change the PF value — only the assignment changes.
> 3. **Applying formula to unbalanced loads:** $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$ is **ONLY for balanced loads**. For unbalanced loads, you must compute $\phi$ from individual phase impedances.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Active power in W (or kW), reactive in VAR (or kVAR), apparent in VA (or kVA). Mixing them loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Delta vs Star confusion:** For delta load, $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$ and $V_{ph} = V_L$. For star load, $I_{ph} = I_L$ and $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] The **two-wattmeter method** works for **three-wire, three-phase systems** (no neutral needed).
- [x] Based on **Blondel's Theorem** ($n-1$ wattmeters for $n$-wire system).
- [x] $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ - \phi)$ — current in R, voltage $V_{RB}$.
- [x] $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ + \phi)$ — current in Y, voltage $V_{YB}$.
- [x] $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ — **always true**.
- [x] $Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)$ — **only for balanced loads**.
- [x] $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$ — **only for balanced loads**.
- [x] At **unity PF**: $W_1 = W_2$ (both equal, positive).
- [x] At **PF = 0.5 lagging**: $W_2 = 0$.
- [x] At **PF < 0.5 lagging**: $W_2 < 0$ (negative, requires meter reversal).
- [x] **Phase sequence effect**: RYB + lagging PF → $W_1 > W_2$. RBY + lagging PF → $W_2 > W_1$.
- [x] **Leading PF** reverses the role: $W_2 > W_1$ in RYB, $W_1 > W_2$ in RBY.
- [x] **Apparent power**: $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$.
- [x] **Power factor types**: $\cos\phi$ (displacement PF), $\dfrac{P}{S}$ (true PF). For sinusoidal balanced systems, both are equal.
- [x] **Pre-lab reading**: Always verify the **rotating direction** of phase sequence indicator before connecting wattmeters.
- [x] **Post-lab calculation**: Always compute the **vector sum** of $W_1$ and $W_2$ at $60^\circ$ to cross-verify total power: $P_{resultant} = \sqrt{W_1^2 + W_2^2 + W_1 W_2}$.
- [x] **Safety**: Ensure the variac is at zero output before switching ON; never exceed the rated current of wattmeter current coils (typically 5 A or 10 A in lab).
- [x] **Lab Viva Question**: *"Why does the $W_2$ reading become negative at low PF?"* — Because $\cos(30^\circ + \phi)$ becomes negative when $\phi > 60^\circ$, i.e., $\cos\phi < 0.5$.
- [x] **Lab Viva Question**: *"Can two wattmeters measure power in a 4-wire system?"* — **No**, not directly. You need either three wattmeters or one wattmeter plus a neutral connection for measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Three Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL307 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on Three Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Three Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor (SCIM)** is a self-starting, robust, brushless AC rotating machine in which the rotor consists of a laminated cylindrical core with conducting bars (usually aluminium) embedded in semi-closed slots, short-circuited at both ends by end-rings — hence the visual resemblance to a "squirrel cage". The stator carries a balanced three-phase distributed winding that produces a **rotating magnetic field** (RMF) when energised from a balanced three-phase AC supply.

The **Load Test** on a three-phase squirrel cage induction motor is a direct, full-load experimental procedure in which the motor is mechanically coupled to a controllable artificial load (typically a **rope-brake / prony-brake / hydraulic dynamometer / DC generator**) and is operated from rated voltage and frequency. The input electrical quantities (line voltage, line current, power) and output mechanical quantities (speed, shaft torque) are measured at successive load steps from no-load to about 125 % of rated load. From these readings, the **complete performance characteristics** — *slip, speed, torque, output power, input power, power factor, and efficiency* — are evaluated and plotted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (verbatim)**
> The experiment is officially listed as *"Load test on three-phase squirrel cage induction motor to obtain its performance characteristics."* Students must use the exact phrase *"performance characteristics"* when writing aims in the record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedal-Boat on a Circular Pond

Imagine a circular pond with a strong water current flowing in a circle (this is the **rotating magnetic field**). A small pedal-boat (the **squirrel cage rotor**) is placed in the pond. Because the boat is light and the current is strong, the boat is dragged around — it tries to chase the current but can never catch up. The small difference in speed between the water current and the boat is exactly the **slip**. Now, if passengers climb into the boat (this is the **mechanical load**), the boat slows down slightly — the slip increases — and the drag force on the boat increases to push the heavier boat. This drag is the **electromagnetic torque** developed by the motor. By measuring how much weight the boat can pull, how fast it goes, and how much "energy" the current supplies, we get a complete picture of the boat's performance — which is exactly what a load test does for the induction motor.

### 1.3 Why This Experiment is in the KTU Lab Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> The squirrel cage motor is the **workhorse of industry** — over **80 %** of all industrial motors worldwide are squirrel cage induction motors. KTU mandates this experiment because it teaches a student to:
> 1. Verify manufacturer-rated performance against actual laboratory readings.
> 2. Calculate **efficiency, slip, power factor, and torque** simultaneously from a single experiment.
> 3. Develop practical skill in connecting a 3-phase motor, applying a controllable mechanical load, and using analog/digital meters safely.
> 4. Plot the **performance curves** — a skill repeatedly tested in KTU viva voce.

> [!WARNING]
> **Standard KTU Safety Note:** The rotor must be firmly coupled, the brake-drum cooled with water (in rope-brake tests) before applying full load, and the starter must be in the lowest tap before switching on the supply.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of a squirrel cage induction motor can be broken into five KTU-exam-friendly logical steps:

1. **Three-phase stator winding is energised.** When a balanced 3-phase sinusoidal supply ($V_L$, $f$ Hz) is applied, currents displaced by **120° electrical** flow in the three-phase windings.
2. **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) is produced.** The combined effect of the three pulsating fields is a constant-amplitude magnetic field that rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s$.
3. **Rotor conductors cut the RMF.** The rotating flux links the short-circuited rotor bars; by Faraday's law, an EMF is induced in them.
4. **Rotor current flows and produces torque.** Since the bars are short-circuited, large currents flow; these currents in the stator RMF produce a **Lorentz force** ($F = BIL \sin\theta$) on every bar. The sum of these tangential forces is the **electromagnetic torque** ($T_e$).
5. **Rotor accelerates but never reaches $N_s$.** If $N_r = N_s$, relative velocity = 0, induced EMF = 0, torque = 0. Therefore the rotor must *slip* by a small percentage. The **slip $s$** is the fractional difference between $N_s$ and $N_r$.

### 2.2 Equivalent-Circuit Perspective (Why Load Test Gives All Quantities)

The exact per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor (referred to stator) is:

$$V_1 \;=\; E_1 \;=\; I_1\!\left(R_1 + jX_1\right) + I_2'\!\left(\dfrac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'\right)$$

The mechanical load on the shaft manifests electrically as a change in the **effective rotor resistance** $R_2'/s$. As the load torque increases, slip $s$ increases, the reflected rotor impedance decreases, the rotor current increases, and the stator current drawn from the supply also increases. By simply measuring stator-side quantities ($V_L$, $I_L$, $P_{in}$) and rotor speed, we can compute every internal quantity.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examiner-favourite and appear in nearly every KTU Part-A and Part-B question on this experiment.** Memorise the table column-by-column.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / When to Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply frequency (Hz), $P$ = number of poles (integer) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit or % | Direct from measured $N_r$ using a tachometer |
| 3 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ | rpm | Used to plot speed–load curve |
| 4 | Input power (2-wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | W (or kW) | Two-wattmeter method is the KTU standard for 3-phase balanced load |
| 5 | Power factor (2-wattmeter) | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L}$ | dimensionless | Always positive for an induction motor |
| 6 | Shaft output torque | $T_{sh} = (F_1 - F_2)\,R \times 9.81$ | N·m | For rope-brake test; $F_1, F_2$ in kgf, $R$ in metres |
| 7 | Shaft output power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ | W | Convert $N_r$ in rpm to rad/s |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Direct from measured values |
| 9 | Mechanical (friction + windage) loss | $P_{mech} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_0}{60}$ | W | $T_0$ = no-load torque from no-load run |
| 10 | Stator copper loss | $P_{sc1} = 3 I_1^2 R_1$ | W | Per-phase $R_1$ from DC resistance test |
| 11 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rc} = s \times P_g$ | W | $P_g$ = air-gap power |
| 12 | Air-gap power | $P_g = \dfrac{P_{rc}}{s} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot 2\pi N_s}{60}$ | W | Power transferred across air gap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note for KTU Record:** When writing $\vert s \vert$ or $\vert I \vert$ in the record, always write `\vert s \vert` or use plain text. The pipe symbol `|` inside a markdown table column **breaks** the table renderer. This same rule was applied throughout the cheat sheet above.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Motor selection for industrial drives:** The slip at full load, rated torque, and breakdown torque — all obtained from the load test — decide whether a SCIM is suitable for constant-speed applications (lathes, fans, compressors) or variable-load applications (conveyors, crushers).
* **Energy auditing:** Industrial plants save crores of rupees annually by replacing under-loaded inefficient motors with high-efficiency ones identified through such load-test data.
* **Condition monitoring:** A sudden increase in slip at no extra load indicates rotor bar failure; a drop in efficiency indicates bearing wear or stator winding degradation. This is the basis of modern **motor-current signature analysis (MCSA)** used in predictive maintenance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Two-Wattmeter Method, Power Factor & Input Power (KTU Favourite)

**Given:** A 3-phase, 3-wire balanced load with line voltage $V_L$, line current $I_L$, and wattmeter readings $W_1$ and $W_2$.

**Step 1 — Total real power.** For a balanced 3-phase load, the sum of the two wattmeter readings gives the total active (real) power drawn:

$$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$$

**Reason:** The two-wattmeter method works for any 3-phase, 3-wire system (with or without neutral) because it measures the instantaneous product of line voltages and line currents in two phases; their sum equals the total real power consumed in all three phases.

**Step 2 — Power factor from the two readings.** Derive from the geometry of the two-wattmeter phasor diagram. Let $\phi$ be the phase angle between phase voltage and phase current. Then:

$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ - \phi)$$
$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30^\circ + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the readings.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_1 + W_2 &= V_L I_L \bigl[\cos(30^\circ - \phi) + \cos(30^\circ + \phi)\bigr] \\
&= V_L I_L \bigl[2\cos 30^\circ \cos\phi\bigr] \\
&= 2 V_L I_L \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos\phi \\
&= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for power factor.**

$$\cos\phi = \dfrac{W_1 + W_2}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L}$$

**KTU Exam Tip:** The induction motor has a *lagging* power factor (always). If both $W_1$ and $W_2$ are positive → p.f. $> 0.5$ (under-load region). If one wattmeter reads negative (reverse its connection and put a minus sign) → p.f. $< 0.5$ (very light load). The cross-over p.f. = **0.5** is a classic viva question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Slip, Synchronous Speed and Rotor Copper Loss

**Given:** Number of poles $P$, supply frequency $f$, measured rotor speed $N_r$ (in rpm).

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed.** A 2-pole RMF rotates at $60f$ revolutions per second. Each additional pair of poles halves the speed. With $P$ poles, the number of pole-pairs is $P/2$, so:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

**Step 2 — Slip.** Slip is the relative speed normalised by the synchronous speed:

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Step 3 — Rotor copper loss from air-gap power.** The air-gap power $P_g$ is the total power crossing the air gap from stator to rotor. It is split between rotor copper loss $P_{rc}$ and mechanical power developed $P_m$:

$$P_g = P_{rc} + P_m$$

From rotor-circuit theory, the slip-dependent effective resistance is $R_2'/s$. The total air-gap power is the power dissipated in this effective resistance:

$$P_g = 3\,(I_2')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$

The actual rotor copper loss is the power in the *true* rotor resistance:

$$P_{rc} = 3\,(I_2')^2 \cdot R_2'$$

**Step 4 — Ratio.**

$$\dfrac{P_{rc}}{P_g} = \dfrac{3 (I_2')^2 R_2'}{3 (I_2')^2 R_2'/s} = s$$

Therefore:

$$P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g$$

This is one of the most elegant and most-asked derivations in the KTU ESE — remember the **"rotor Cu loss = slip × air-gap power"** identity.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Rope-Brake (Prony-Brake) Torque Equation

**Given:** A rope wound around a brake-drum of radius $R$ (metres) on the motor shaft. The two ends of the rope are attached to spring balances reading $F_1$ (tight side) and $F_2$ (slack side) in kgf.

**Step 1 — Net tangential force.** The net force pulling the drum in the direction of rotation is:

$$F_{net} = (F_1 - F_2) \;\; \text{kgf}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Newtons.** $1\ \text{kgf} = 9.81\ \text{N}$, so:

$$F_{net} = 9.81\,(F_1 - F_2) \;\; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Torque = force × radius.**

$$T_{sh} = 9.81\,(F_1 - F_2)\,R \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft output power.**

$$P_{out} = T_{sh}\,\omega_r = T_{sh}\,\dfrac{2\pi N_r}{60} \;\; \text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative loading methods** for squirrel-cage motors (where rotor terminals are *not* accessible, so direct electrical loading is impossible):
> 1. **Rope brake** — cheapest, used in KTU labs (water-cooled rope).
> 2. **Prony brake** — same principle, with an adjustable lever.
> 3. **Hydraulic / eddy-current dynamometer** — most accurate, used in industry.
> 4. **Coupled DC generator** — load the DC generator with a rheostat; the SCIM acts as the prime mover. This is the most common **indirect loading** method in modern labs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Calculation Roadmap

The following matrix is the **exam-day computation flow** that a KTU student must follow after recording the readings:

| Step | Measured / Known | Compute | Formula | Output Column in Tabulation |
|------|------------------|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $P$, $f$ | $N_s$ | $N_s = 120f/P$ | rpm |
| 2 | $N_r$ (tachometer) | $s$ | $s = (N_s-N_r)/N_s$ | p.u. |
| 3 | $V_L$, $I_L$, $W_1$, $W_2$ | $P_{in}$ | $W_1+W_2$ | W |
| 4 | $V_L$, $I_L$, $P_{in}$ | p.f. | $P_{in}/(\sqrt{3}V_LI_L)$ | — |
| 5 | $F_1$, $F_2$, $R$ | $T_{sh}$ | $9.81(F_1-F_2)R$ | N·m |
| 6 | $N_r$, $T_{sh}$ | $P_{out}$ | $2\pi N_r T_{sh}/60$ | W |
| 7 | $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$ | $\eta$ | $P_{out}/P_{in} \times 100$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU record write-up rule: present the calculation table in the **same column order as the observation table**. Examiners award **2 marks** for the **formula** and **2 marks** for the **substitution step** — *write them out even if you are confident*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains3A[3 Phase AC Mains 415 V 50 Hz] --> tpf[TPTP Autotransformer Starter]
    tpf --> v1[Voltmeter V1 Line Voltage]
    tpf --> amm1[Ammeter A1 Line Current]
    tpf --> wm1[Wattmeter W1]
    tpf --> wm2[Wattmeter W2]
    tpf --> motor1[Three Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor]
    motor1 --> coupling1[Mechanical Coupling]
    coupling1 --> brakeDrum[Brake Drum with Rope and Spring Balances]
    brakeDrum --> springF1[Spring Balance F1 Tight Side]
    brakeDrum --> springF2[Spring Balance F2 Slack Side]
    motor1 --> tacho1[Tachometer for Speed N_r]
    brakeDrum --> waterCool[Water Cooling for Drum]
    springF1 --> torqueCompute[Compute Shaft Torque T_sh]
    springF2 --> torqueCompute
    tacho1 --> slipCompute[Compute Slip s]
    wm1 --> powerCompute[Compute P_in and Power Factor]
    wm2 --> powerCompute
    v1 --> powerCompute
    amm1 --> powerCompute
    torqueCompute --> effCompute[Compute Efficiency eta]
    powerCompute --> effCompute
    slipCompute --> perfPlot[Plot Performance Curves]
    effCompute --> perfPlot
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Flow — Reading-Taking Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of Experiment]) --> checkA[Check all connections and earth link]
    checkA --> noLoad[Run motor at rated voltage, no load, record V I W1 W2 N_r and F1 F2]
    noLoad --> incLoad1[Increase load step 1 apply small brake tension]
    incLoad1 --> rec1[Record all meter and balance readings]
    rec1 --> incLoad2[Increase load step 2 and step 3 etc up to 125 percent rated]
    incLoad2 --> monitorA{Stator temperature and current within limits}
    monitorA --> no[Stop and cool]
    monitorA --> yes[Continue to next step]
    yes --> recN[Record final reading]
    recN --> decLoad[Gradually release load to zero]
    decLoad --> stopMotor[Switch off supply using starter in lowest tap]
    stopMotor --> tabulate[Tabulate readings and compute performance]
    tabulate --> plot1[Plot Speed vs Output]
    plot1 --> plot2[Plot Torque vs Output]
    plot2 --> plot3[Plot Efficiency vs Output]
    plot3 --> plot4[Plot Slip vs Output]
    plot4 --> plot5[Plot Power Factor vs Output]
    plot5 --> endA([Experiment Complete])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Performance Curve Shapes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xaxis[Output Power P_out X Axis] --> yN[Speed N_r Y Axis]
    xaxis --> yT[Shaft Torque T_sh Y Axis]
    xaxis --> yEta[Efficiency eta Y Axis]
    xaxis --> yS[Slip s Y Axis]
    xaxis --> yPF[Power Factor cos phi Y Axis]
    yN --> curve1[Speed drops very slightly nearly straight line close to N_s]
    yT --> curve2[Torque rises linearly from small no load torque]
    yEta --> curve3[Efficiency rises steeply peaks at about 80 percent load then drops]
    yS --> curve4[Slip rises almost linearly with load]
    yPF --> curve5[Power factor starts very low no load rises steadily peaks at rated load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shapes described above are the **qualitative** performance curves a KTU examiner expects in the viva. Be ready to sketch them freehand: **N_r falls slightly, T_sh rises linearly, η peaks at ~80 % full load, s rises linearly, p.f. rises from ~0.1 at no-load to ~0.85 at full load.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q1. What is a squirrel cage induction motor? Why is it called "squirrel cage"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A squirrel cage induction motor is a three-phase AC rotating machine whose rotor consists of a laminated cylindrical core with conducting bars (usually aluminium) embedded in parallel slots and short-circuited at both ends by two conducting end-rings. *(1 mark)* The construction resembles the revolving cage used by squirrels in pet cages, hence the name. *(1 mark)* The rotor has no windings, no slip rings and no brushes, making it extremely robust, cheap and almost maintenance-free. *(1 mark)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q2]**

**Q2. Define slip of an induction motor. What is its typical value at full load for a standard industrial SCIM?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Slip is defined as the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction (or percentage) of the synchronous speed. *(2 marks)*

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

For a standard industrial squirrel-cage induction motor, the full-load slip is typically **2 % to 5 %**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q10(a) Modified]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to conduct a load test on a three-phase squirrel cage induction motor using a rope-brake loading arrangement. List the instruments used and the precautions to be observed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Circuit / Block Diagram:** *(2 marks)* — Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1. The components are: 3-phase autotransformer starter, voltmeter (across lines), ammeter (in any one line), two wattmeters (two-wattmeter method), the SCIM, mechanical coupling to brake drum, two spring balances ($F_1$ and $F_2$), tachometer, and a water-cooling arrangement for the drum.
2. **Procedure:** *(3 marks)*
   - Make connections as per the diagram, ensure proper earthing.
   - Keep the autotransformer in the **zero-voltage position** and switch ON the supply.
   - Gradually increase the voltage to rated value using the autotransformer.
   - Allow the motor to run at **no-load**; record $V_L$, $I_L$, $W_1$, $W_2$, $N_r$, $F_1$, $F_2$.
   - Apply load in **5–6 steps** up to ~125 % rated by tightening the rope.
   - At each step, record all the above readings.
   - Gradually release the load, bring the autotransformer back to zero and switch OFF.
3. **Instruments and Precautions:** *(2 marks)* — Tachometer (digital/optical), two spring balances (0–10 kgf), rope, water flow, double-pole switch, MCCB / fuse. Precautions: earthing, water cooling before applying full load, current limit check, no-load reading for friction loss.

---

**Q. (b)** The following readings were taken during the load test of a 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole squirrel cage induction motor using the two-wattmeter method and a rope brake of effective drum radius 0.2 m.

| Load step | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | $N_r$ (rpm) | $F_1$ (kgf) | $F_2$ (kgf) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 (No-load) | 400 | 4.5 | 350 | −150 | 1495 | 0.3 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 400 | 6.0 | 600 | 200 | 1480 | 1.5 | 0.4 |
| 3 (Rated) | 400 | 8.5 | 1050 | 850 | 1465 | 3.6 | 0.6 |
| 4 | 400 | 10.5 | 1380 | 1280 | 1450 | 5.2 | 0.7 |

Calculate for **each load step**: (i) input power, (ii) power factor, (iii) shaft torque, (iv) output power, and (v) efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given constants:** $P = 4$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $R = 0.2\ \text{m}$.

Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$

**Load Step 1 (No-load):**
- (i) $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 350 + (-150) = 200\ \text{W}$. *(½ mark)*
- (ii) $\cos\phi = \dfrac{200}{\sqrt{3}\times 400 \times 4.5} = \dfrac{200}{3117.7} = 0.064$. *(½ mark)*
- (iii) $T_{sh} = 9.81 \times (0.3-0.2) \times 0.2 = 0.1962\ \text{N·m}$. *(½ mark)*
- (iv) $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1495 \times 0.1962}{60} = 30.7\ \text{W}$. *(½ mark)*
- (v) $\eta = \dfrac{30.7}{200} \times 100 = 15.4\%$. *(½ mark)*

**Load Step 2:**
- (i) $P_{in} = 600 + 200 = 800\ \text{W}$.
- (ii) $\cos\phi = \dfrac{800}{\sqrt{3}\times 400 \times 6.0} = \dfrac{800}{4156.9} = 0.192$.
- (iii) $T_{sh} = 9.81 \times (1.5-0.4) \times 0.2 = 2.158\ \text{N·m}$.
- (iv) $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1480 \times 2.158}{60} = 334.4\ \text{W}$.
- (v) $\eta = \dfrac{334.4}{800} \times 100 = 41.8\%$.

**Load Step 3 (Rated):**
- (i) $P_{in} = 1050 + 850 = 1900\ \text{W}$.
- (ii) $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1900}{\sqrt{3}\times 400 \times 8.5} = \dfrac{1900}{5888.5} = 0.323$.
- (iii) $T_{sh} = 9.81 \times (3.6-0.6) \times 0.2 = 5.886\ \text{N·m}$.
- (iv) $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1465 \times 5.886}{60} = 903.0\ \text{W}$.
- (v) $\eta = \dfrac{903.0}{1900} \times 100 = 47.5\%$.

**Load Step 4:**
- (i) $P_{in} = 1380 + 1280 = 2660\ \text{W}$.
- (ii) $\cos\phi = \dfrac{2660}{\sqrt{3}\times 400 \times 10.5} = \dfrac{2660}{7274.6} = 0.366$.
- (iii) $T_{sh} = 9.81 \times (5.2-0.7) \times 0.2 = 8.829\ \text{N·m}$.
- (iv) $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1450 \times 8.829}{60} = 1340.4\ \text{W}$.
- (v) $\eta = \dfrac{1340.4}{2660} \times 100 = 50.4\%$.

*(1 mark for correct formula usage, 1 mark for proper units and tabulation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q. (a)** Explain, with the help of the approximate equivalent circuit, why the rotor copper loss of an induction motor equals the slip times the air-gap power. State the engineering significance of this identity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The per-phase approximate equivalent circuit places the magnetising branch at the input terminals. After referring the rotor to the stator, the rotor loop contains the **true rotor resistance $R_2'$** in series with a resistance $R_2'(1-s)/s$ that represents the mechanical load, the total divided by slip $s$ giving the effective resistance $R_2'/s$. *(2 marks)*

The current in the rotor branch is:

$$I_2' = \dfrac{E_1}{\sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + (X_2')^2}}$$

The **air-gap power** (total power crossing from stator to rotor) is the power dissipated in $R_2'/s$:

$$P_g = 3\,(I_2')^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$$

The **rotor copper loss** is the power dissipated in the *true* $R_2'$ only:

$$P_{rc} = 3\,(I_2')^2 \cdot R_2'$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{P_{rc}}{P_g} = s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g \quad \text{(proved)}$$

*(2 marks for derivation.)*

**Engineering Significance:** *(3 marks)*
1. The slip $s$ decides how much of the air-gap power is *lost as heat* in the rotor bars — a higher-slip motor is inherently less efficient.
2. High-starting-torque applications (e.g., lifts, cranes) use **high-resistance rotor bars** (deep-bar or double-cage design) — this increases starting torque *and* running losses.
3. Normal industrial squirrel-cage motors are designed with low rotor resistance so that $s$ at full load is only 2–5 %, giving high efficiency.
4. This identity is the basis of the **induction generator** — when driven *above* synchronous speed, slip becomes negative, rotor Cu loss becomes negative (i.e., power flows *out* of the rotor into the stator) and the machine delivers power to the grid.

---

**Q. (b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole squirrel-cage induction motor runs at 960 rpm at full load. Calculate: (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip, (iii) rotor copper loss per phase if the air-gap power is 6 kW, and (iv) the rotor frequency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$, $P = 6$, $N_r = 960\ \text{rpm}$, $P_g = 6\ \text{kW}$.

**(i) Synchronous speed:** *(2 marks)*

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\ \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Slip:** *(2 marks)*

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1000 - 960}{1000} = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**(iii) Rotor copper loss (total and per phase):** *(2 marks)*

$$P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g = 0.04 \times 6000 = 240\ \text{W (total)}$$

Per phase (3-phase balanced):

$$P_{rc,\phi} = \dfrac{240}{3} = 80\ \text{W}$$

**(iv) Rotor frequency:** *(1 mark)*

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2\ \text{Hz}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this experiment (verified against examiner reports):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on $W_2$** at light load. If $W_2$ is negative (reading taken with reversed connection), put a *minus* in the observation table — never drop the sign. *(−1 to −2 marks if missed.)*
> 2. **Mixing units in torque calculation.** $F$ in kgf must be multiplied by **9.81** to get N; $R$ must be in **metres**, not centimetres. *(−1 mark if wrong units.)*
> 3. **Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$ in the $P_{out}$ formula.** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ uses the **actual rotor speed**, not synchronous speed. This is one of the most common errors; examiners report up to **20 % of scripts** carry this mistake. *(−2 marks.)*
> 4. **Not plotting the curves.** KTU record rules require **all five performance curves** (Speed, Torque, Slip, Efficiency, Power Factor vs Output Power) — missing two or more costs **2 marks** outright.
> 5. **Power factor at no load is very low (~0.1)**. Many students write a value of 0.8 because they confuse the no-load test with the rated-load value. Show the calculation step explicitly.
> 6. **Brake-drum cooling water must be mentioned** in the procedure. Skipping this safety line costs a mark in the safety section.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Squirrel cage induction motor** = robust, brushless, self-starting, most-used industrial motor.
- **Load test purpose:** obtain *complete* performance characteristics from a single experiment.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — depend only on supply frequency and number of poles, *not* on load.
- **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$. For a normal industrial SCIM, full-load $s$ = 2–5 %.
- **Two-wattmeter method** is the KTU-standard way to measure 3-phase power. $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$, p.f. = $P_{in}/(\sqrt{3} V_L I_L)$.
- **Shaft torque (rope brake):** $T_{sh} = 9.81(F_1 - F_2)R$ N·m. Output power uses the *measured* $N_r$, not $N_s$.
- **Key identity:** $P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g$ — rotor copper loss = slip × air-gap power. Foundation of induction generator operation.
- **Rotor frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — at full load typically 1–2 Hz, used in slip-ring rotor resistance design.
- **Performance curve shapes:** $N_r$ falls slightly, $T_{sh}$ rises almost linearly, $s$ rises linearly, $p.f.$ rises from ~0.1 (no-load) to ~0.85 (full-load), $\eta$ peaks at ~80 % full-load.
- **Indirect loading** (coupled DC generator) is preferred for squirrel-cage motors in modern labs because the rotor is *sealed* and slip rings are not accessible for direct electrical loading.
- **Precautions to always mention:** proper earthing, autotransformer at zero before switching on, brake-drum water-cooling before full load, do not exceed rated current by more than 25 %, release load gradually before switch-off.
- **KTU viva favourites:** "Why is power factor low at no-load?" (magnetising current dominates), "Why is starting current high?" (rotor at standstill, $s = 1$, reflected resistance very low), "Can a SCIM run at synchronous speed?" (No — zero slip, zero torque).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Open Circuit (OC) Test** and **Short Circuit (SC) Test** are the two indirect, no-load (non-loading) experiments performed on a single-phase transformer to determine its equivalent circuit parameters, losses, and performance characteristics without actually loading the transformer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> *The OC test is conducted on the LV side with the HV side open, at rated voltage and frequency, to determine the no-load power factor, no-load current, core (iron) loss, and the shunt branch parameters (magnetizing reactance $X_m$ and core loss resistance $R_c$). The SC test is conducted on the HV side with the LV side short-circuited, by applying a small reduced voltage to circulate rated full-load current, to determine the copper loss at full load and the equivalent series parameters (equivalent resistance $R_{01}$ and equivalent leakage reactance $X_{01}$).*

- **OC Test → Shunt Branch Parameters** (parallel branch of the equivalent circuit)
- **SC Test → Series Branch Parameters** (lumped series impedance of the equivalent circuit)
- **Combined Output → Equivalent Circuit, Efficiency, and Voltage Regulation at any load and power factor**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Blood Test" of a Transformer)

Imagine a doctor wants to check the health of a human patient without performing major surgery. Instead, the doctor prescribes two simple **blood tests**:

| Medical Analogy | Transformer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Fasting Blood Test** (patient at rest, body doing minimal work) | **OC Test** (transformer energized but secondary delivers no current; the iron core is the only "organ" working) |
| **Stress Test / Treadmill Test** (body forced to work at full capacity via a small trigger) | **SC Test** (a small applied voltage pushes the transformer to circulate full-load current, exercising the copper windings) |
| **Combined Blood Report** → Doctor diagnoses overall health | **Combined OC + SC data** → Engineer determines complete equivalent circuit and efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a single blood report alone cannot diagnose a patient fully (you need both fasting and post-meal readings), a single test on a transformer is insufficient. The **OC test reveals the iron heart** of the machine, while the **SC test reveals the copper veins**. Together, they form a complete medical record of the transformer — hence, KTU insists that **both** tests be performed and analyzed in Module Experiment 2.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Rated Frequency:** $f = 50\text{ Hz}$ (India, KTU standard)
- **Rated Voltage:** $V_1$ on the energized winding (LV side for OC, HV side for SC)
- **Rated Apparent Power:** $S_{\text{rated}}$ in kVA
- **Ambient Reference Temperature:** $25^\circ\text{C}$ for standard parameter calculation
- **Power Factor:** $\cos\phi_0$ (OC) and $\cos\phi_{sc}$ (SC) — dimensionless
- **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\text{ rad/s}$ for $f = 50\text{ Hz}$

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — Why and How

A single-phase transformer, although physically comprising two windings on a common magnetic core, can be **mathematically represented by an equivalent circuit** that references all quantities to either the primary or secondary side. The key engineering question is: *How do we obtain the values of the four essential parameters — $R_{01}, X_{01}, R_0, X_0$ (or $R_c, X_m$)?*

### A. The Open Circuit (OC) Test — Step-by-Step Logic

> **Why the LV side?**
> Because the LV winding has a lower rated voltage, applying rated voltage to it is safer and more convenient (meters and variac operate at safer voltages). Also, the no-load current is small (typically 2–10% of rated current), so the LV side instrument readings are more accurate.

> **Why measure power?**
> Because at no-load, the input power equals the **iron/core loss** ($P_i$ or $P_c$). The copper loss in the primary at no-load is negligible ($I_0^2 R_1 \approx 0$, since $I_0$ is very small).

**Step 1:** Keep the HV side open (no load connected). Connect the LV side to a variac (auto-transformer), voltmeter, ammeter, and wattmeter.

**Step 2:** Slowly increase the variac output until the voltmeter reads the **rated LV voltage** $V_{1,\text{rated}}$.

**Step 3:** Record the readings:
- $V_0 = V_{1,\text{rated}}$ (voltmeter)
- $I_0$ (ammeter) — the no-load current
- $W_0$ (wattmeter) — the input power

**Step 4:** Calculate derived quantities:

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 \cdot I_0}$$

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 \quad \text{(working/component — current through } R_c \text{)}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 \quad \text{(magnetizing component — current through } X_m \text{)}$$

$$R_c = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{V_0^2}{W_0}$$

$$X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu}$$

**The 'Why' of each quantity:**
- $I_w$ is the in-phase current that supplies the iron loss; the wattmeter reads $V_0 \cdot I_w = W_0$.
- $I_\mu$ is the quadrature current that establishes the alternating flux in the core.
- $R_c$ and $X_m$ are in **parallel** (the shunt branch of the equivalent circuit).

### B. The Short Circuit (SC) Test — Step-by-Step Logic

> **Why the HV side?**
> The HV winding carries the **lower rated current** compared to the LV winding. By energizing the HV side and shorting the LV side, the meters (ammeter, voltmeter, wattmeter) operate at high voltage but low current, which is more accurate and within instrument ratings.

> **Why a reduced voltage?**
> If full HV rated voltage were applied with the secondary shorted, the current would be dangerously high (10–20 times rated), burning the windings. The applied voltage is gradually raised only until the ammeter reads **rated full-load current** $I_{1,\text{rated}}$.

**Step 1:** Short-circuit the LV side through a thick copper strip (or ammeter). Connect the HV side to a variac, voltmeter, ammeter, and wattmeter.

**Step 2:** Slowly increase the variac output until the ammeter reads **rated HV current** $I_{1,\text{rated}}$.

**Step 3:** Record the readings:
- $V_{sc}$ (voltmeter) — typically 5–10% of rated voltage
- $I_{sc} = I_{1,\text{rated}}$ (ammeter)
- $W_{sc}$ (wattmeter) — the input power

**Step 4:** Calculate derived quantities:

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

$$R_{01} = Z_{01} \cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

$$X_{01} = Z_{01} \sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

**The 'Why' of each quantity:**
- $Z_{01}$ is the total equivalent impedance referred to the HV side.
- $R_{01}$ is the lumped equivalent resistance ($R_1 + R_2'$), so $W_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 R_{01}$ represents the **full-load copper loss**.
- $X_{01}$ is the lumped equivalent leakage reactance ($X_1 + X_2'$).
- During SC test, the applied voltage is so small that the core flux is negligible, hence **iron loss is zero**; the wattmeter reads only the **copper loss** at full-load current.

### C. Performance Predictions Using the Two Tests

Once the equivalent circuit is known, the efficiency and voltage regulation at any fractional load $x$ and power factor $\cos\phi$ are:

$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{FL}}}$$

where $P_i = W_0$ and $P_{cu,\text{FL}} = W_{sc}$.

$$\text{Voltage Regulation} = \frac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi \pm X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

(+ for lagging PF, − for leading PF)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Source Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | No-load power factor | $\cos\phi_0 = \dfrac{W_0}{V_0 I_0}$ | dimensionless | OC |
| 2 | Core loss component of current | $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | Ampere (A) | OC |
| 3 | Magnetizing component of current | $I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ | Ampere (A) | OC |
| 4 | Core loss resistance | $R_c = \dfrac{V_0^2}{W_0}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | OC |
| 5 | Magnetizing reactance | $X_m = \dfrac{V_0}{I_\mu}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | OC |
| 6 | Short circuit power factor | $\cos\phi_{sc} = \dfrac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} I_{sc}}$ | dimensionless | SC |
| 7 | Equivalent impedance (referred) | $Z_{01} = \dfrac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | SC |
| 8 | Equivalent resistance (referred) | $R_{01} = \dfrac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | SC |
| 9 | Equivalent reactance (referred) | $X_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) | SC |
| 10 | Iron loss | $P_i = W_0$ | Watt (W) | OC |
| 11 | Full-load copper loss | $P_{cu,\text{FL}} = W_{sc}$ | Watt (W) | SC |
| 12 | Efficiency at load fraction $x$ | $\eta = \dfrac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}}$ | per unit (p.u.) | Combined |
| 13 | Voltage regulation (lagging PF) | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi + X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Combined |
| 14 | Voltage regulation (leading PF) | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{I(R_{01}\cos\phi - X_{01}\sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Combined |
| 15 | Condition for maximum efficiency | $x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}} = P_i \Rightarrow x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,\text{FL}}}$ | load fraction | Combined |
| 16 | Turns ratio (for parameter transfer) | $a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | dimensionless | Rated Nameplate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The *referred-to* subscript ($R_{01}$, $X_{01}$, $Z_{01}$) means the value is on the side where the test was performed. If parameters are to be used on the other side, divide by $a^2$. Most KTU problems ask you to retain them as referred values to whichever side you measured on.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transformer Design Validation:** Manufacturers use OC and SC tests to verify that the design specifications (losses, impedance, efficiency) are met before dispatch.
- **Acceptance Testing:** Every power transformer installed in substations undergoes these tests as part of **pre-commissioning** checks.
- **Lifetime Aging Studies:** Periodic SC tests reveal whether winding resistance has increased (indicating loose connections or overheating damage).
- **Protection Relay Setting:** The $Z_{01}$ value determines the **impedance seen by distance relays**, critical for transmission line protection coordination.
- **Loss Capitalization:** Utilities use the iron loss (always present) and copper loss (load-dependent) to compute the **cost of losses** over the transformer's life — a major factor in tender evaluation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Phasor Diagram to Equivalent Circuit

### Derivation 1: Deriving $R_c$ and $X_m$ from OC Test Data

**Given:** OC test readings $V_0$, $I_0$, $W_0$.

**Step 1: Establish the no-load phasor relationships.**

The applied voltage $V_0$ is taken as the reference phasor along the real axis. The no-load current $I_0$ lags $V_0$ by a small angle $\phi_0$ (the no-load power factor angle). The phasor diagram consists of:

- $\vec{V_0}$ along the real axis.
- $\vec{I_0}$ lagging $\vec{V_0}$ by $\phi_0$.
- $\vec{I_w} = I_0 \cos\phi_0$ — the in-phase component along the real axis.
- $\vec{I_\mu} = I_0 \sin\phi_0$ — the quadrature component along the negative imaginary axis (lagging $V_0$ by 90°).

**Step 2: Resolve $I_0$ into orthogonal components.**

Using basic trigonometry on the phasor triangle:

$$I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2}$$

The power measured by the wattmeter is the product of voltage and the in-phase current:

$$W_0 = V_0 \cdot I_w = V_0 \cdot I_0 \cos\phi_0$$

**Step 3: Solve for the no-load power factor angle.**

Rearranging the wattmeter equation:

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 \cdot I_0}$$

**Step 4: Compute the two orthogonal current components.**

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = I_0 \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_0}$$

**Step 5: Apply Ohm's law to the parallel shunt branch.**

Since $R_c$ carries the current $I_w$ at voltage $V_0$:

$$R_c = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{V_0^2}{W_0}$$

Since $X_m$ carries the current $I_\mu$ at voltage $V_0$:

$$X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu}$$

**Result:** The two shunt branch parameters are determined purely from OC test readings, without loading the transformer.

---

### Derivation 2: Deriving $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ from SC Test Data

**Given:** SC test readings $V_{sc}$, $I_{sc}$, $W_{sc}$.

**Step 1: Recognize that the short-circuited transformer behaves as a simple series R-L circuit.**

With the secondary shorted, the only opposition to current flow is the lumped series impedance $Z_{01} = R_{01} + jX_{01}$. The applied voltage $V_{sc}$ drives the current $I_{sc}$ through this impedance.

**Step 2: Apply Ohm's law in magnitude form.**

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}}$$

**Step 3: Apply power balance.**

In an R-L series circuit, the wattmeter reading is the power dissipated in the resistance:

$$W_{sc} = I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{01}$$

Solving for $R_{01}$:

$$R_{01} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2}$$

**Step 4: Compute the power factor angle of the short-circuit test.**

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the leakage reactance using the impedance triangle.**

From the impedance triangle ($Z_{01}$ as hypotenuse, $R_{01}$ as adjacent, $X_{01}$ as opposite):

$$X_{01} = Z_{01} \sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{Z_{01}^2 - R_{01}^2}$$

**Step 6: Express $\sin\phi_{sc}$ explicitly (used by KTU examiners).**

$$\sin\phi_{sc} = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi_{sc}} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} I_{sc}}\right)^2}$$

**Result:** The series parameters are determined purely from SC test readings. Since the applied voltage is small (5–10% of rated), the core flux is negligible, so iron loss contribution to $W_{sc}$ is virtually zero.

---

### Derivation 3: Efficiency Equation from OC and SC Data

**Starting point:** The output power of a transformer at fractional load $x$ and power factor $\cos\phi$ is:

$$P_{\text{out}} = x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi$$

where $S_{\text{rated}}$ is the rated apparent power in VA (or kVA).

**Losses at fractional load:**
- Iron loss: $P_i = W_0$ (constant, independent of load, since core flux is essentially constant from no-load to full-load).
- Copper loss at fractional load: $P_{cu} = (I_0 \cdot x)^2 \cdot R_{01} = x^2 \cdot I_{sc}^2 \cdot R_{01} = x^2 \cdot W_{sc}$.

**Input power:**

$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} + P_i + x^2 \cdot W_{sc}$$

**Efficiency definition:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi + W_0 + x^2 \cdot W_{sc}}$$

**Special case — maximum efficiency:**

To find the load $x$ at which $\eta$ is maximum, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero. The result is the classical condition:

$$P_i = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{FL}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,\text{FL}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{W_0}{W_{sc}}}$$

At this optimum load, the iron loss equals the copper loss, and the maximum efficiency is:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi + 2 W_0}$$

---

### Derivation 4: Voltage Regulation Using the Approximate Method

**Approximate voltage regulation formula (KTU standard):**

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{I_1 (R_{01} \cos\phi \pm X_{01} \sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100$$

**Sign convention:**
- **Lagging PF:** Use $(+)$ because $I_1 R_{01}$ drop is in phase with $V_2$, and the reactive drop $I_1 X_{01}$ adds to it in the worst case.
- **Leading PF:** Use $(-)$ because the leading current's reactive drop partially cancels the resistive drop.

**Detailed phasor justification:** Refer to the phasor diagram where $\vec{V_1} = \vec{V_2} + \vec{I_1}(R_{01} + jX_{01})$. The magnitude of $\vec{V_1}$ is found by adding $\vec{V_2}$ and the impedance drop vectorially. For small regulation values (a few percent), the approximation holds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A 5 kVA, 200 V / 400 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer gave the following test results:

- **OC Test (LV side energized):** $V_0 = 200\text{ V}$, $I_0 = 0.75\text{ A}$, $W_0 = 75\text{ W}$
- **SC Test (HV side energized):** $V_{sc} = 18\text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 12.5\text{ A}$, $W_{sc} = 110\text{ W}$

Determine:
1. The equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side.
2. The efficiency at 80% of full load and 0.8 power factor lagging.
3. The maximum efficiency and the load at which it occurs.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Part 1: Equivalent Circuit Parameters**

**Step 1.1: Compute the turns ratio.**

$$a = \frac{V_{\text{HV}}}{V_{\text{LV}}} = \frac{400}{200} = 2$$

**Step 1.2: OC test — compute shunt branch parameters (referred to LV side).**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 \cdot I_0} = \frac{75}{200 \times 0.75} = \frac{75}{150} = 0.5$$

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 0.75 \times 0.5 = 0.375\text{ A}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 0.75 \times \sin(\cos^{-1}(0.5)) = 0.75 \times 0.866 = 0.6495\text{ A}$$

$$R_c \text{ (LV side)} = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{200}{0.375} = 533.33\ \Omega$$

$$X_m \text{ (LV side)} = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu} = \frac{200}{0.6495} = 307.93\ \Omega$$

**Step 1.3: OC parameters referred to HV side (divide by $a^2 = 4$).**

$$R_c \text{ (HV side)} = \frac{533.33}{4} = 133.33\ \Omega$$

$$X_m \text{ (HV side)} = \frac{307.93}{4} = 76.98\ \Omega$$

**Step 1.4: SC test — compute series parameters (referred to HV side).**

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{110}{18 \times 12.5} = \frac{110}{225} = 0.4889$$

$$Z_{01} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{18}{12.5} = 1.44\ \Omega$$

$$R_{01} = Z_{01} \cos\phi_{sc} = 1.44 \times 0.4889 = 0.704\ \Omega$$

$$X_{01} = Z_{01} \sin\phi_{sc} = 1.44 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.4889^2} = 1.44 \times 0.8724 = 1.256\ \Omega$$

**Part 2: Efficiency at 80% Load, 0.8 PF Lagging**

$$P_{\text{out}} = 0.8 \times 5000 \times 0.8 = 3200\text{ W}$$

$$P_i = W_0 = 75\text{ W}$$

$$P_{cu} = (0.8)^2 \times W_{sc} = 0.64 \times 110 = 70.4\text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{in}} = 3200 + 75 + 70.4 = 3345.4\text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{3200}{3345.4} = 0.9565 = 95.65\%$$

**Part 3: Maximum Efficiency**

$$x_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{W_0}{W_{sc}}} = \sqrt{\frac{75}{110}} = \sqrt{0.6818} = 0.8257$$

**At $x_{\max}$, the load is 82.57% of full load.**

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{x_{\max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x_{\max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + 2 W_0}$$

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{0.8257 \times 5000 \times 0.8}{0.8257 \times 5000 \times 0.8 + 2 \times 75} = \frac{3302.8}{3302.8 + 150} = \frac{3302.8}{3452.8} = 0.9566 = 95.66\%$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- $R_{01} = 0.704\ \Omega$, $X_{01} = 1.256\ \Omega$ (HV side)
- $R_c = 133.33\ \Omega$, $X_m = 76.98\ \Omega$ (HV side)
- $\eta_{0.8,\ 0.8\ \text{PF}} = 95.65\%$
- $\eta_{\max} = 95.66\%$ at $x = 0.8257$ (i.e., 82.57% of full load)
