# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

*Why is the open circuit test performed on the low voltage side of a transformer?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Each Point Carries 1 Mark):**

1. **Lower Voltage Safety:** The LV side has a lower rated voltage, so applying rated voltage to it is safer for the operator and within the insulation and instrument ratings. **(1 mark)**

2. **Accurate Current Measurement:** The no-load current on the LV side is relatively higher (in absolute terms) than on the HV side, allowing the ammeter to give a more accurate and stable reading. **(1 mark)**

3. **Practical Convenience:** Standard lab variacs and meters are designed for low-voltage operation, making the LV-side measurement practically convenient. **(1 mark)**

> [!NOTE]
> Equivalent reversed question: *"Why is the SC test performed on the HV side?"* — Answer with mirror logic: lower rated current on HV side, accurate ammeter reading, safety with high voltage but small current.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*In a short circuit test on a transformer, why is the iron loss neglected?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Low Applied Voltage:** In the SC test, the applied voltage is only 5–10% of the rated voltage, and since $V \propto \phi$ (flux is directly proportional to voltage), the core flux is correspondingly very small. **(1 mark)**

2. **Iron Loss is Flux-Dependent:** Core (iron) loss consists of hysteresis loss ($P_h \propto f \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6}$) and eddy current loss ($P_e \propto B_{\max}^2$). Both depend on the square (or higher) of the flux density, which is drastically reduced at low applied voltage. **(1 mark)**

3. **Wattmeter Reads Only Copper Loss:** Consequently, the wattmeter reading $W_{sc}$ represents essentially only the **full-load copper loss** $I_{sc}^2 R_{01}$, with iron loss contribution being negligibly small (less than 0.25% of rated). **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

A 10 kVA, 1000 V / 200 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer gave the following test results:

- **OC Test (LV side open, HV energized):** $V_0 = 1000\text{ V}$, $I_0 = 0.5\text{ A}$, $W_0 = 200\text{ W}$
- **SC Test (HV side open, LV side shorted):** $V_{sc} = 50\text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 50\text{ A}$, $W_{sc} = 400\text{ W}$

**(a)** Determine the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the efficiency and voltage regulation of the transformer at 75% of full load and 0.85 power factor lagging. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit Parameters (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the turns ratio.**

$$a = \frac{V_{\text{HV}}}{V_{\text{LV}}} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 2: OC test calculations — to find $R_c$ and $X_m$ (referred to HV side directly).**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 \cdot I_0} = \frac{200}{1000 \times 0.5} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0 = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.2\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$I_\mu = I_0 \sin\phi_0 = 0.5 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.4^2} = 0.5 \times 0.9165 = 0.4583\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$R_c = \frac{V_0}{I_w} = \frac{1000}{0.2} = 5000\ \Omega = 5\text{ k}\Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_m = \frac{V_0}{I_\mu} = \frac{1000}{0.4583} = 2181.95\ \Omega = 2.182\text{ k}\Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 3: SC test calculations — to find $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ (referred to LV side; will be transferred to HV side).**

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{W_{sc}}{V_{sc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{400}{50 \times 50} = \frac{400}{2500} = 0.16 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$Z_{02} = \frac{V_{sc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$R_{02} = \frac{W_{sc}}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{400}{2500} = 0.16\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_{02} = \sqrt{Z_{02}^2 - R_{02}^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.0256} = \sqrt{0.9744} = 0.9871\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Refer the series parameters to the HV side (multiply by $a^2 = 25$).**

$$R_{01} = a^2 \cdot R_{02} = 25 \times 0.16 = 4.0\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_{01} = a^2 \cdot X_{02} = 25 \times 0.9871 = 24.68\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Final Equivalent Circuit (HV side referred):**
- $R_c = 5000\ \Omega$, $X_m = 2181.95\ \Omega$ (shunt branch)
- $R_{01} = 4.0\ \Omega$, $X_{01} = 24.68\ \Omega$ (series branch)

---

#### Part (b) — Efficiency and Voltage Regulation at 75% Load, 0.85 PF Lagging (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the output power.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = x \cdot S_{\text{rated}} \cdot \cos\phi = 0.75 \times 10000 \times 0.85 = 6375\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2: Compute losses.**

$$P_i = W_0 = 200\text{ W (iron loss, constant)} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$P_{cu} = x^2 \cdot W_{sc} = (0.75)^2 \times 400 = 0.5625 \times 400 = 225\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3: Compute total input and efficiency.**

$$P_{\text{in}} = 6375 + 200 + 225 = 6800\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$\eta = \frac{6375}{6800} = 0.9375 = 93.75\% \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Compute voltage regulation.**

Full-load current on HV side:

$$I_{1,\text{FL}} = \frac{S_{\text{rated}}}{V_{\text{HV}}} = \frac{10000}{1000} = 10\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

Actual current at 75% load:

$$I_1 = 0.75 \times 10 = 7.5\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

For $\cos\phi = 0.85$ lagging, $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = 0.5268$.

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{I_1 (R_{01} \cos\phi + X_{01} \sin\phi)}{V_1} \times 100 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{7.5 \times (4.0 \times 0.85 + 24.68 \times 0.5268)}{1000} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{7.5 \times (3.4 + 13.001)}{1000} \times 100$$

$$\text{VR}\% = \frac{7.5 \times 16.401}{1000} \times 100 = \frac{123.01}{1000} \times 100 = 12.30\% \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = 93.75\%$
- **Voltage Regulation:** $\text{VR} = 12.30\%$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

A 2 kVA, 400 V / 100 V, 50 Hz, single-phase transformer underwent the following tests:

- **OC Test (LV side):** $V_0 = 100\text{ V}$, $I_0 = 0.6\text{ A}$, $W_0 = 30\text{ W}$
- **SC Test (HV side):** $V_{sc} = 20\text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 5\text{ A}$, $W_{sc} = 40\text{ W}$

**(a)** Find the equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side and draw the phasor diagram at no-load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the load at which maximum efficiency occurs and calculate the maximum efficiency at unity power factor. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit Parameters & Phasor Diagram (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Turns ratio.**

$$a = \frac{V_{\text{HV}}}{V_{\text{LV}}} = \frac{400}{100} = 4 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 2: OC test (LV side).**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{W_0}{V_0 I_0} = \frac{30}{100 \times 0.6} = 0.5 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$I_w = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$I_\mu = 0.6 \times \sin(\cos^{-1}(0.5)) = 0.6 \times 0.866 = 0.5196\text{ A} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$R_c \text{ (LV)} = \frac{100}{0.3} = 333.33\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_m \text{ (LV)} = \frac{100}{0.5196} = 192.45\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Transferring to HV side (divide by $a^2 = 16$):**

$$R_c \text{ (HV)} = \frac{333.33}{16} = 20.83\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_m \text{ (HV)} = \frac{192.45}{16} = 12.03\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 3: SC test (HV side directly).**

$$\cos\phi_{sc} = \frac{40}{20 \times 5} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4 \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$Z_{01} = \frac{20}{5} = 4\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$R_{01} = 4 \times 0.4 = 1.6\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$X_{01} = 4 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = 4 \times 0.9165 = 3.666\ \Omega \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4: No-load phasor diagram (description).**

$\vec{V_0}$ along real axis (length 100 V to scale). $\vec{I_0}$ lagging $\vec{V_0}$ by $\phi_0 = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$. $\vec{I_w}$ in phase with $\vec{V_0}$ (0.3 A scale). $\vec{I_\mu}$ perpendicular to $\vec{V_0}$ lagging (0.5196 A scale). The diagonal of the rectangle gives $\vec{I_0}$ (0.6 A). **(0.5 mark for diagram description)**

**Final Equivalent Circuit (HV side):** $R_c = 20.83\ \Omega$, $X_m = 12.03\ \Omega$, $R_{01} = 1.6\ \Omega$, $X_{01} = 3.666\ \Omega$. **(0.5 mark for final summary)**

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum Efficiency (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Condition for maximum efficiency.**

The load fraction at which maximum efficiency occurs:

$$x_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{cu,\text{FL}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{W_0}{W_{sc}}} = \sqrt{\frac{30}{40}} = \sqrt{0.75} = 0.866 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum efficiency occurs at **86.6% of full load**, i.e., at an apparent power of $0.866 \times 2 = 1.732\text{ kVA}$.

**Step 2: At maximum efficiency, iron loss equals copper loss.**

$$P_i = 30\text{ W} \quad \text{and} \quad x_{\max}^2 \cdot W_{sc} = 0.75 \times 40 = 30\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark — verification)}$$

**Step 3: Compute output power at maximum efficiency and unity power factor.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = x_{\max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi = 0.866 \times 2000 \times 1.0 = 1732\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Compute total losses.**

$$P_{\text{losses}} = P_i + x_{\max}^2 \cdot W_{sc} = 30 + 30 = 60\text{ W} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5: Compute maximum efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_{\text{losses}}} = \frac{1732}{1732 + 60} = \frac{1732}{1792} = 0.9665 = 96.65\% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_{\max} = 96.65\%$ at $x = 0.866$ of full load at unity power factor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to refer parameters (3-mark penalty):** Many students compute OC parameters on LV side and SC parameters on HV side, then mix them in the efficiency formula. Always bring all parameters to the **same side** (usually HV) before using them in the regulation or efficiency equations.
> 
> 2. **Sign error in voltage regulation (2-mark penalty):** The plus sign is for **lagging** PF and the minus sign is for **leading** PF. Mixing these up gives an absurdly small or even negative regulation.
> 
> 3. **Wrong numerator in $R_c$ formula (1-mark penalty):** $R_c = V_0^2 / W_0$ (not $V_0 / W_0$). This is one of the most repeated mistakes in KTU answer sheets.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to square the load fraction in copper loss (2-mark penalty):** Copper loss is $x^2 \cdot P_{cu,\text{FL}}$, not $x \cdot P_{cu,\text{FL}}$. A common slip.
> 
> 5. **Not labeling units in final answer (1-mark penalty):** KTU strictly deducts marks if Ohms, Watts, and percentages are not explicitly written.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **OC Test** = LV side energized, HV side **open**, rated voltage, measures **iron loss** ($W_0$) → gives $R_c$ and $X_m$ (shunt branch).
- **SC Test** = HV side energized, LV side **shorted**, reduced voltage, measures **copper loss** ($W_{sc}$) at rated current → gives $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ (series branch).
- **No-load PF** $\cos\phi_0$ is **small and lagging** (typically 0.2 to 0.5) — a heavily inductive operation due to magnetizing current dominance.
- **Short-circuit PF** $\cos\phi_{sc}$ is also small and lagging (typically 0.1 to 0.3) — a heavily reactive operation due to large leakage reactance.
- **Iron loss is constant** from no-load to full-load; **copper loss varies as the square of the load**.
- **Maximum efficiency condition:** $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}$, so $x = \sqrt{W_0 / W_{sc}}$.
- **Voltage regulation is positive for lagging PF**, can be **negative for leading PF** (Ferranti-like effect).
- **Refer all parameters to the same side** before combining — divide by $a^2$ when transferring from HV to LV; multiply by $a^2$ when transferring from LV to HV.
- **Turns ratio** $a = V_1 / V_2 = N_1 / N_2 = I_2 / I_1$ — whichever side has higher voltage is side 1 (primary) in step-down transformers.
- **Equivalent circuit in exact form** has $R_1$ and $R_2'$ separated, but for OC/SC analysis, KTU always uses the **approximate (lumped) equivalent circuit** with $R_{01}$ and $X_{01}$ on one side.
- **Instrument connections:** Voltmeter is connected **directly across the winding** (parallel); Ammeter is in **series**; Wattmeter has its current coil in series and voltage coil in parallel.
- **Phasor diagram at no-load** is a small right triangle with $\vec{V_0}$ as hypotenuse direction, $\vec{I_w}$ along $V_0$, and $\vec{I_\mu}$ perpendicular.
- **KTU practical lab tip:** In the actual lab, always perform the OC test **first** to avoid residual magnetism effects; wait 2–3 minutes between tests for thermal stabilization; and always start the variac from **zero** before increasing to prevent inrush transients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Test on Three Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL307 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load Test on Three Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Load Test on Three Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Phase Slip Ring Induction Motor (SRIM):** A wound-rotor induction machine in which the rotor winding terminals are brought out through slip rings and brushes to an external stationary resistance bank, enabling closed-loop control of starting current, starting torque, and running speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Test:** A direct (poormeter) method of testing in which a mechanical load of known magnitude is applied to the motor shaft, while simultaneously measuring electrical input quantities (voltage, current, power) and mechanical output quantities (speed, torque, slip), to evaluate the **performance characteristics** of the machine under actual loading conditions.

The load test is the most **authentic** performance evaluation technique because, unlike the no-load and blocked-rotor tests (which are indirect), it directly captures the real-world operating behaviour of the motor across the entire load range — from no-load to overload.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Gear-Box Bicycle" on a Hill**
>
> Imagine pushing a loaded bicycle up a steep hill.
> - **Squirrel Cage Motor** = Single-gear bicycle: Pedal hard the moment you start (low starting torque, high starting current).
> - **Slip Ring Motor** = Multi-gear bicycle with adjustable gears: You begin on **gear-1** (high resistance → high torque → smooth climb), and as the cycle gathers momentum, you **shift up gear-by-gear** (reduce external resistance) until you cruise at top gear with minimum effort.
>
> The **slip rings + external rheostat** are the **gear-shifting mechanism** of the induction motor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard quantities** are central to this experiment:

| Physical Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $N_s$ | **r.p.m.** (revolutions per minute) |
| Rotor (actual) speed | $N_r$ | **r.p.m.** |
| Per-unit slip | $s$ | dimensionless (or %) |
| Electromagnetic torque | $T_e$ | **N·m** (Newton-metre) |
| Shaft output torque | $T_{sh}$ | **N·m** |
| Supply frequency | $f$ | **Hz** |
| Number of poles | $P$ | dimensionless |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The external rotor resistance starter used in the slip ring motor is called a **Liquid Resistance Starter (LRS)** in industrial setups and a **Slip-Ring Starter / Rotor Resistance Starter** in laboratory setups. It is the *defining feature* distinguishing the SRIM from the squirrel-cage counterpart.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire experiment revolves around **one fundamental principle**: *The electromagnetic torque developed by an induction motor depends on the rotor circuit resistance and slip.*

**Step 1 — Three-phase stator supply energised.**
A balanced 3-phase supply ($V_L$, $f$ Hz) is applied to the stator winding. A rotating magnetic field is produced that travels around the stator bore at **synchronous speed** $N_s$.

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{[r.p.m.]}$$

**Step 2 — Rotor flux-cutting induces EMF.**
The rotating stator flux cuts the stationary rotor conductors at slip frequency $sf$, inducing an EMF (and hence current) in the wound rotor circuit.

$$E_2 = s \cdot E_2^{BLOCKED}$$

> where $E_2^{BLOCKED}$ is the rotor-induced EMF per phase when the rotor is held stationary.

**Step 3 — External resistance added via slip rings.**
The rotor current flows out through the slip rings → brushes → external rheostat → back into rotor winding. The total rotor circuit resistance becomes $R_2 + R_{ext}$, where $R_{ext}$ is adjustable.

**Step 4 — Torque developed.**
The interaction between stator field and rotor current produces torque proportional to the *product* of rotor flux and the *in-phase component* of rotor current.

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2 \pi N_s} \cdot \dfrac{s \cdot E_2^{2} \cdot R_2^{'}}{(R_2^{'} + R_{ext}^{'})^2 + (s X_2^{'})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why external resistance matters (The "Why"):**
> Maximum torque is independent of rotor resistance, but the **slip at which maximum torque occurs is directly proportional to rotor resistance**. So by adding $R_{ext}$, we shift the *maximum torque point* to higher slip values — meaning we get **maximum starting torque at $s = 1$**, and we can smoothly accelerate the motor from start to full speed.

**Step 5 — Mechanical loading.**
A mechanical brake (rope-and-drum brake / eddy-current dynamometer / pony brake) applies a controllable frictional load on the motor shaft. As load increases, rotor slows down slightly → slip increases → rotor EMF increases → rotor current increases → more torque to balance the load.

**Step 6 — Quasi-steady-state measurement.**
At each load setting, after transients settle, we record: $V_L$, $I_L$, input watts $W_1, W_2$ (for 2-wattmeter method), spring balance readings $S_1, S_2$, rotor speed $N_r$.

## 2.2 Why is This Topic Important? — Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> 1. **Cranes and hoists** — high starting torque required to lift heavy loads from rest.
> 2. **Elevators / lifts** — smooth, jerk-free acceleration essential for passenger comfort.
> 3. **Rolling mills, cement mills, ball mills** — heavy inertial loads need controlled acceleration.
> 4. **Conveyor belts** with varying loads — needs torque boost at low speeds.
> 5. **Pumps and compressors** with high static head — need starting torque > load torque.
>
> The load test **characterises the motor for these exact duties**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield formulas — reproduce verbatim in KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | r.p.m. |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit (pu) |
| 3 | Slip percentage | $s_\% = s \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | r.p.m. |
| 5 | Net shaft torque (rope brake) | $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot (R - r) \cdot 9.81$ | N·m |
| 6 | Output mechanical power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60}$ | watts |
| 7 | Input power (3-phase, 2-wattmeter) | $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ | watts |
| 8 | Input line current (average) | $I_L = \dfrac{I_{R} + I_{Y} + I_{B}}{3}$ | A |
| 9 | Power factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | W |
| 12 | Mechanical power developed | $P_{mech} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}$ | W |
| 13 | Air-gap power | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot 2 \pi N_s}{60}$ | W |
| 14 | Starting torque ratio | $T_{st} \propto \dfrac{R_2^{'} + R_{ext}^{'}}{R_2^{'}}$ | factor |

> **Where the symbols mean:**
> $S_1, S_2$ — spring balance readings (kgf); $R$ — brake drum radius (m); $r$ — rope radius (m); $W_1, W_2$ — wattmeter readings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed from Supply Frequency

The rotating magnetic field completes one electrical cycle in time $T = 1/f$ seconds. In a $P$-pole machine, one full *mechanical* revolution of the field corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles.

$$\text{Time for one mechanical revolution of field} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = \dfrac{60}{T_{mech}} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** 1 Hz = 1 cycle/sec; 1 minute = 60 sec → multiply by 60 to convert rev/sec → rev/min.

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip of an Induction Motor

Let $N_r$ be the actual rotor speed. By definition:

$$s = \dfrac{\text{Relative speed between rotating field and rotor}}{\text{Field speed}}$$

$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = 1 - \dfrac{N_r}{N_s}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{N_r = N_s (1 - s)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Shaft Torque from Rope-and-Drum Brake

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU labs** and must be memorised perfectly.

A rope is wound around the brake drum of radius $R$. Two spring balances $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side) hold the rope. The net tangential force on the drum creates a braking torque opposing rotation.

**Step 1:** Net force exerted by rope (in kgf):

$$F_{net} = S_1 - S_2 \quad \text{[kgf]}$$

**Step 2:** Effective radius for torque calculation. Because the rope has thickness $r$, the rope's *centre-line* lies at radius $(R + r)$ from the drum centre.

$$T_{brake} = F_{net} \cdot (R + r) \quad \text{[kgf·m]}$$

**Step 3:** Convert kgf to N by multiplying by $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$:

$$\boxed{T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot (R + r) \cdot 9.81 \quad \text{[N·m]}}$$

> **Note (KTU Pitfall):** Many students write $T = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot R \cdot 9.81$ — this **loses 2 marks** in valuation because the rope thickness is overlooked.

## 3.4 Derivation: Output Power in Watts

Mechanical power is the rate of doing work. Torque $T_{sh}$ acts at angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r}{60}$ rad/s.

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega = T_{sh} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N_r}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r \, T_{sh}}{60} \quad \text{[W]}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Air-Gap Power, Rotor Cu Loss, and Mechanical Power Split

Cross-multiplying the power flow:

$$P_{gap} : P_{rcu} : P_{mech} \;\; = \;\; 1 : s : (1 - s)$$

> This is the **single most important relationship** in induction motor theory. Derivation:

The air-gap power is the power transferred to the rotor circuit. Of this, $s$-fraction is lost as rotor copper loss, and $(1-s)$-fraction is converted to mechanical form.

**Step 1:** Rotor copper loss = $I_2^{'2} \cdot R_2^{'}$ where $I_2^{'} = \dfrac{s \, E_2^{'}}{\sqrt{(R_2^{'})^{2} + (s X_2^{'})^{2}}}$.

**Step 2:** At small slips (operating region), $s X_2^{'} \ll R_2^{'}$, hence $I_2^{'} \approx \dfrac{s \, E_2^{'}}{R_2^{'}}$, giving $I_2^{'2} \propto s^{2}$.

**Step 3:** Per-phase air-gap power $P_{gap,ph} = E_2^{'} \cdot I_2^{'} \cdot \cos \phi_2 \propto s$.

**Step 4:** Hence rotor Cu loss $\propto s \cdot P_{gap} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ and mechanical power $\propto (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}$.

$$\boxed{P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{gap} \quad ; \quad P_{mech} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Calculation Walkthrough — A Solved Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example (typical KTU-style 14-mark data):**
>
> A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, slip-ring induction motor gave the following readings during a load test with a rope-and-drum brake: $V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$, $I_L = 12 \, \text{A}$, $W_1 + W_2 = 5800 \, \text{W}$, $N_r = 1410 \, \text{r.p.m.}$, spring balances: $S_1 = 8 \, \text{kgf}$, $S_2 = 1.5 \, \text{kgf}$, drum radius $R = 10 \, \text{cm}$, rope radius $r = 1 \, \text{cm}$. Find: slip, shaft torque, output, efficiency, power factor.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step (a): Synchronous speed**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{r.p.m.}$$

**Step (b): Slip**

$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1410}{1500} = \dfrac{90}{1500} = 0.06 \; (\text{i.e., } 6\%)$$

**Step (c): Shaft torque**

$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \cdot (R + r) \cdot 9.81$$

$$T_{sh} = (8 - 1.5) \cdot (0.10 + 0.01) \cdot 9.81$$

$$T_{sh} = 6.5 \times 0.11 \times 9.81 = 7.013 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step (d): Output power**

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N_r \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 1410 \cdot 7.013}{60}$$

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{62{,}128.5}{60} = 1035.5 \, \text{W} \; (\approx 1.035 \, \text{kW})$$

**Step (e): Power factor**

$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L} = \dfrac{5800}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 12}$$

$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{5800}{8625.0} = 0.6725 \; (\text{lagging})$$

**Step (f): Efficiency**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1035.5}{5800} \times 100 = 17.85\%$$

> The **low efficiency** is because we are operating at low slip and reduced external rotor resistance; in practice, external $R_{ext}$ is *removed* at full load so efficiency improves. At this loaded point, slip is small and rotor $I^{2}R$ losses are minimal, but the *current* is high due to load, causing stator copper losses.

**Step (g): Rotor copper loss and air-gap power**

Assuming stator losses $P_{stator} = P_{in} - P_{gap}$. For a typical machine, $P_{gap} \approx P_{in} - P_{stator Cu loss} - P_{core}$, but a **simpler approximation** in lab calculations:

$$P_{gap} \approx P_{in} - \text{stator Cu loss}$$

If we take stator Cu loss as 200 W (assumed), then $P_{gap} = 5600 \, \text{W}$.

$$P_{rcu} = s \cdot P_{gap} = 0.06 \cdot 5600 = 336 \, \text{W}$$

$$P_{mech} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{gap} = 0.94 \cdot 5600 = 5264 \, \text{W}$$

$$\text{Shaft output} = P_{mech} - \text{friction \& windage}$$

If friction and windage are say 150 W, then $P_{sh} = 5264 - 150 = 5114 \, \text{W}$, and $\eta = 5114 / 5800 = 88.2\%$, which is realistic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two efficiency values are possible** depending on the assumed constant losses. The first (17.85%) used **directly measured output via rope brake**, while the second (~88%) used **indirect calculation via slip**. KTU accepts both methods; the choice depends on whether the lab manual specifies **direct** or **indirect** calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Load Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3-Phase AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> B[TPST Main Switch with Fuses]
    B --> C[3-Phase Variac Autotransformer Optional]
    C --> D[Voltmeter VL and Ammeters IR IY IB]
    D --> E[Stator Terminal Box of Slip Ring Induction Motor]
    E --> F[SRIM Stator Winding 3-Phase Distributed]
    G[3-Phase Variac for Rotor OR DC Exciter] --> H[External Rotor Rheostat 3-Phase Bank]
    F --> I[SRIM Rotor Winding Wound]
    I --> J[Slip Rings 3 Nos Mounted on Shaft]
    J --> K[Carbon Brushes Stationary]
    K --> H
    H --> L[Shorting Link For Full Speed Operation]
    F --> M[Coupling to Brake Drum]
    M --> N[Rope Wound Around Drum]
    N --> O[Spring Balance S1 Tight Side]
    N --> P[Spring Balance S2 Slack Side]
    M --> Q[Tachometer for Speed Measurement]
    R[Wattmeter 1 W1] --> E
    S[Wattmeter 2 W2] --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **stator** is fed directly (or through a variac) from the 3-phase mains.
> - The **rotor winding** is connected via **slip rings → brushes → external rheostat**, which is *gradually cut out* as the motor accelerates.
> - The **brake drum** is mechanically coupled to the shaft; the rope exerts braking force measured by spring balances.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Operational Steps During the Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1 Inspect Motor and Identify Slip Rings] --> Step2[Step 2 Set External Rotor Resistance to MAXIMUM]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Apply 3-Phase Rated Voltage to Stator]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Motor Starts Smoothly High Starting Torque Low Starting Current]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Gradually Cut Out Rotor Resistance Section by Section]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Reach Full Speed with Shorting Link ON]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7 Apply No Load Read all Meters Record NL Readings]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8 Apply Load Step by Step Using Brake Drum Tightening Nut]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9 For Each Load Record VL IL W1 W2 S1 S2 Speed]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 10 Continue Until Full Load or Stalling]
    Step10 --> Step11[Step 11 Reduce Load to Zero Switch Off Supply]
    Step11 --> Step12[Step 12 Tabulate and Compute Performance Curves]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Performance Characteristics to Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Recorded Observations per Load Setting] --> Q1[Calculate Slip s]
    P --> Q2[Calculate Shaft Torque Tsh]
    P --> Q3[Calculate Output Power Pout]
    P --> Q4[Calculate Power Factor cos phi]
    P --> Q5[Calculate Efficiency percent]
    P --> Q6[Calculate Line Current IL]
    Q1 --> R1[Graph 1 Slip versus Output]
    Q2 --> R2[Graph 2 Speed versus Torque]
    Q3 --> R3[Graph 3 Output versus Torque]
    Q4 --> R4[Graph 4 Power Factor versus Output]
    Q5 --> R5[Graph 5 Efficiency versus Output]
    Q6 --> R6[Graph 6 Line Current versus Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandatory Graphs (usually 5 out of these 6 are asked):**
> 1. Speed ($N_r$) vs Output ($P_{out}$)
> 2. Slip ($s$) vs Output ($P_{out}$)
> 3. Efficiency ($\eta$) vs Output ($P_{out}$)
> 4. Power Factor ($\cos \phi$) vs Output ($P_{out}$)
> 5. Line Current ($I_L$) vs Output ($P_{out}$)
