<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1a]**
>
> **Q1.** *Why is a slip ring induction motor preferred over a squirrel cage motor for high-starting-torque applications such as hoists and cranes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A slip ring induction motor has its rotor winding brought out through **slip rings and brushes** to an **external rheostat**. By adding external resistance to the rotor circuit at starting, the starting torque is increased (up to maximum torque value) and starting current is reduced simultaneously. This is not possible in a squirrel cage motor, whose rotor resistance is fixed and low. Hence, for **high-starting-torque** applications such as hoists, cranes, and elevators, the SRIM is preferred. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1b]**
>
> **Q2.** *Define slip of an induction motor and state its typical value at full load for a slip-ring induction motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Slip $s$ of an induction motor is defined as the **difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed**:
$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
For a slip-ring induction motor operating at full load with **no external rotor resistance**, the typical value of slip is between **3% and 5%**. When external resistance is connected, slip increases proportionally to maintain torque balance. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Slip Ring Motor Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting rope thickness:** The torque formula $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2)(R + r) \cdot 9.81$ — students who omit the $+r$ term **lose 2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Using synchronous speed instead of rotor speed in output formula:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r T_{sh}}{60}$ requires **rotor speed**, not $N_s$. Cost = 2 marks.
> 3. **Not converting kgf to N:** Forgetting to multiply by 9.81 yields torque in kgf·m; **conversion is mandatory** in SI units.
> 4. **Confusing air-gap power with input power:** In indirect efficiency calculation, $P_{in} - P_{stator Cu} - P_{core} = P_{gap}$, *not* $P_{in} = P_{gap}$.
> 5. **Skipping the unit of slip (%):** Always mention whether slip is in pu or %.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2a]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct a **load test on a three-phase slip-ring induction motor** using a **rope-and-drum brake**. List the instruments used and the readings taken.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** From the following data of a load test on a 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor, calculate the **slip, shaft torque, output power, efficiency, and power factor** at full load:
>
> - Line voltage $V_L = 415 \, \text{V}$
> - Line current $I_L = 15 \, \text{A}$
> - Total input power $W_1 + W_2 = 7800 \, \text{W}$
> - Rotor speed $N_r = 1425 \, \text{r.p.m.}$
> - Spring balance readings: $S_1 = 12 \, \text{kgf}$, $S_2 = 2 \, \text{kgf}$
> - Brake drum radius $R = 7.5 \, \text{cm}$, rope radius $r = 1 \, \text{cm}$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Circuit Diagram and Connections (3 marks):**

A 3-phase supply is connected to the stator of the SRIM through a TPST switch and fuse unit. Voltmeter $V$ is connected across any two lines. Three ammeters $A_1, A_2, A_3$ measure line currents. Two wattmeters $W_1$ and $W_2$ are connected in the **two-wattmeter method** configuration.

The rotor winding terminals are brought out through three slip rings and brushes to a **three-phase external rheostat**. The rheostat has three identical sections, one in each rotor phase. At starting, all resistance is in circuit; as the motor accelerates, the resistance is progressively cut out using a star-connected handle. After full speed, the slip rings are **short-circuited** and brushes may be lifted to reduce wear.

The motor shaft is coupled to a **rope-brake drum**. A hemp rope wound 1.5–2 turns around the drum has its two ends connected to spring balances $S_1$ (tight side) and $S_2$ (slack side). A **tachometer** (digital or mechanical) measures rotor speed.

**Instruments Used (2 marks):**
- Voltmeter (MI type, 0–500 V)
- Three ammeters (MI, 0–30 A)
- Two wattmeters (Dynamometer, 0–1500 W)
- Two spring balances (0–20 kg)
- Tachometer (0–3000 rpm)
- Rope-and-drum brake assembly

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Set the **external rotor resistance to maximum** using the starter handle.
2. Switch on the 3-phase supply; motor starts with **high starting torque and low current**.
3. **Gradually cut out rotor resistance** in steps until full speed is attained.
4. Take **no-load readings** of $V_L$, $I_L$, $W_1, W_2$, speed $N_r$, and spring balances ($S_1 = S_2$ ideally for no-load).
5. Apply load in steps by **tightening the brake-drum nut**; at each step record all meters and the spring balances.
6. Continue until full load; then **gradually release load, switch off**.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Synchronous speed**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \, \text{r.p.m.}$$

**Step 2: Slip**
$$s = \dfrac{1500 - 1425}{1500} = \dfrac{75}{1500} = 0.05 \; (5\%)$$

**Step 3: Shaft torque**
$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2)(R + r) \cdot 9.81$$
$$T_{sh} = (12 - 2) \cdot (0.075 + 0.01) \cdot 9.81$$
$$T_{sh} = 10 \cdot 0.085 \cdot 9.81 = 8.339 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: Output power**
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N_r \cdot T_{sh}}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 1425 \cdot 8.339}{60}$$
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{74{,}660}{60} = 1244.3 \, \text{W} \approx 1.244 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Power factor**
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L} = \dfrac{7800}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 15}$$
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{7800}{10{,}782.6} = 0.7233 \; \text{lagging}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1244.3}{7800} \times 100 = 15.95\%$$

> The low efficiency suggests that either (i) the motor is operating far below rated load, (ii) the assumed external resistance is dissipating significant power in the rotor circuit, or (iii) friction losses dominate. In an actual motor, efficiency at full load is typically **85–92%**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q2b]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working principle of a three-phase slip-ring induction motor** with the help of a labelled diagram. Why are slip rings and brushes used?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The following readings were obtained during the load test of a 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V slip-ring induction motor:
>
> | Load | $V_L$ (V) | $I_L$ (A) | $W_1$ (W) | $W_2$ (W) | $N_r$ (rpm) | $S_1$ (kg) | $S_2$ (kg) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | No load | 400 | 6.5 | 380 | -120 | 980 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
> | ¼ load | 400 | 9.0 | 1850 | 350 | 970 | 4.5 | 1.5 |
> | ½ load | 400 | 12.0 | 2750 | 950 | 955 | 8.0 | 2.0 |
> | ¾ load | 400 | 15.5 | 3550 | 1700 | 940 | 11.5 | 2.5 |
> | Full load | 400 | 19.0 | 4350 | 2400 | 920 | 15.0 | 3.0 |
>
> Given: Drum radius = 10 cm, Rope radius = 1 cm. **Plot the curves of (i) Speed vs Output, (ii) Slip vs Output, (iii) Efficiency vs Output, (iv) Power Factor vs Output.** State two inferences from each curve.

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Construction (4 marks):** Stator — laminated silicon steel stamping slots carrying a 3-phase distributed winding. Rotor — laminated cylinder with slots housing a **3-phase insulated winding** similar to the stator. The three rotor phases are connected in star, and the free ends are soldered to **three copper slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft. Carbon brushes riding on the slip rings connect the rotor winding to an external circuit.

**Working (2 marks):** Stator winding, when fed by 3-phase AC, produces a **rotating magnetic field** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. This field cuts the rotor conductors, inducing an EMF and current in the rotor circuit. The rotor current interacts with the stator field to produce **torque**, accelerating the rotor in the direction of field rotation. The rotor settles at a speed $N_r < N_s$ where developed torque equals load torque.

**Why Slip Rings? (1 mark):** Slip rings allow **external resistance** to be inserted in the rotor circuit. This boosts starting torque, limits starting current, and provides **smooth speed control** below synchronous speed — features absent in squirrel-cage motors.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Sample calculations for full-load row (other rows similar):

**Step 1: Synchronous speed**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \, \text{r.p.m.}$$

**Step 2: Slip (full load)**
$$s = \dfrac{1000 - 920}{1000} = 0.08 \; (8\%)$$

**Step 3: Shaft torque (full load)**
$$T_{sh} = (15 - 3) \cdot (0.10 + 0.01) \cdot 9.81 = 12 \cdot 0.11 \cdot 9.81 = 12.949 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: Output power (full load)**
$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 920 \cdot 12.949}{60} = 1247.8 \, \text{W} \; (1.248 \, \text{kW})$$

**Step 5: Input power (full load)**
$$P_{in} = W_1 + W_2 = 4350 + 2400 = 6750 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 6: Power factor**
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{6750}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 19} = \dfrac{6750}{13{,}164.5} = 0.5128 \; \text{lagging}$$

**Step 7: Efficiency**
$$\eta = \dfrac{1247.8}{6750} \times 100 = 18.49\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above values are computed for the full-load row. Similar calculations must be repeated for each row. Students should tabulate all values and plot the four characteristic curves on **graph paper** with proper axis labels and units.

**Inferences (2 marks):**
1. **Speed vs Output:** Speed decreases with increase in load, but only by a few percent (typical slip 4–8%). This is the **drooping characteristic** of an induction motor.
2. **Slip vs Output:** Slip increases linearly with load, confirming $s \propto T$ in the normal operating region.
3. **Efficiency vs Output:** Efficiency rises sharply at low loads, peaks near rated load, and may fall slightly beyond full load (due to higher copper losses).
4. **Power Factor vs Output:** Power factor is poor at no-load (≈ 0.1–0.2) and improves with load as the active power component increases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Load Test on 3-Phase SRIM**

- **Core objective:** Determine performance characteristics (speed vs load, torque vs load, efficiency vs load, etc.) of a slip-ring induction motor under varying mechanical loads.

- **Defining feature of SRIM:** Rotor winding brought out via **slip rings + brushes + external rheostat**.

- **Why SRIM and not squirrel cage?** Better starting torque, lower starting current, smooth speed control.

- **Brake used:** Rope-and-drum brake (most common in KTU labs); also eddy-current dynamometer, pony brake.

- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$. For a 4-pole, 50 Hz motor: $N_s = 1500$ rpm.

- **Slip formula:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.

- **Shaft torque:** $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2)(R + r) \cdot 9.81$ — *do NOT forget rope radius + 9.81 conversion.*

- **Output power:** $P_{out} = 2\pi N_r T_{sh} / 60$ — *use rotor speed, not synchronous.*

- **Input power (2-wattmeter method):** $P_{in} = W_1 + W_2$ — *ignore the sign of $W_2$ if negative; record magnitude.*

- **Power factor:** $\cos \phi = P_{in}/(\sqrt{3} V_L I_L)$.

- **Efficiency:** $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \cdot 100\%$.

- **Power flow identity:** $P_{gap} = P_{rcu}/s = P_{mech}/(1-s)$. Rotor Cu loss $= s \cdot P_{gap}$; mechanical power $= (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$.

- **Five mandatory graphs:** (i) Speed vs Output, (ii) Slip vs Output, (iii) Efficiency vs Output, (iv) Power Factor vs Output, (v) Line Current vs Output.

- **Safety step:** Always keep external rotor resistance at **maximum during starting**.

- **Slip-rings short-circuited** at full-load running speed for maximum efficiency.

- **Inferences expected in viva:** Why slip cannot be zero, why efficiency drops at very low and very high loads, why starting current is high in squirrel cage but limited in SRIM.

- **Most common mistake:** Using $N_s$ instead of $N_r$ in output power formula — a 2-mark deduction each occurrence.

- **Remember:** A higher external rotor resistance gives **higher starting torque** but **lower running efficiency**; resistance must be cut out during normal running.

- **Final KTU motto for this experiment:** *"Brake the motor, calculate the torque, plot the curves, infer the characteristic — and never forget the rope thickness!"*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Characteristics of SCR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL307 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Static Characteristics of SCR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of SCR Static Characteristics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer (P-N-P-N), three-junction, semiconductor bistable switching device with three externally accessible terminals: the **Anode (A)**, the **Cathode (K)**, and the **Gate (G)**. It conducts current only in one direction (unidirectional) and remains in the OFF state until a proper gate signal is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** *Static characteristics of an SCR* refer to the **steady-state Voltage–Current (V-I) relationship** between the anode-cathode terminals under controlled DC biasing conditions, plotted with the gate terminal either open-circuited or supplied with a fixed gate current. These curves are obtained WITHOUT any switching action or AC operation — purely DC sweep measurements.

The static V-I characteristic is plotted on a graph with:
- **X-axis:** Anode-to-Cathode Voltage, $V_{AK}$ (in Volts)
- **Y-axis:** Anode Current, $I_A$ (in mA or A)

This single plot encapsulates **four distinct operating regions** of the SCR.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Hydraulic Dam with a Control Gate"**
>
> Imagine a massive **dam** holding back a huge reservoir of water (this represents the **anode-to-cathode voltage** that the SCR can block in its OFF state).
>
> - The **main sluice gates** of the dam are **closed** by default — no water flows, no current flows. This is the **Forward Blocking** state.
> - There is a small **leakage trickle** even when the gates are closed — this represents the small **forward leakage current** ($I_{DRM}$).
> - The dam has a **small auxiliary side-valve** (the **Gate terminal**) operated by a worker. When the worker opens this small side-valve, water pressure builds up and automatically forces the **main sluice gates** wide open. This is **Gate Triggering** → SCR turns ON.
> - Once the main gates are fully open, even if the worker shuts the auxiliary valve (removes the gate signal), the dam **stays open** because of the massive water flow. This is **latching**.
> - The water flow will only stop if the main reservoir level drops below a critical minimum. This minimum is the **Holding Current ($I_H$)**.

This is exactly how an SCR behaves: a small gate pulse triggers a massive anode current, and the device latches ON until the current falls below $I_H$.

## 1.3 Key Static Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Breakover Voltage | $V_{BO}$ | **200 V – 2500 V** | Voltage at which SCR turns ON without gate signal |
| Holding Current | $I_H$ | **5 mA – 50 mA** | Minimum anode current to keep SCR ON |
| Latching Current | $I_L$ | **10 mA – 100 mA** | Minimum anode current to latch SCR ON at turn-on |
| On-State Voltage Drop | $V_T$ | **~1.5 V – 2.5 V** | Voltage across SCR when fully conducting |
| Reverse Breakdown Voltage | $V_{BR}$ | **200 V – 2500 V** | Reverse voltage at which SCR avalanches |
| Gate Trigger Current | $I_{GT}$ | **1 mA – 50 mA** | Minimum gate current needed to turn ON |
| Gate Trigger Voltage | $V_{GT}$ | **0.5 V – 3 V** | Gate voltage corresponding to $I_{GT}$ |
| Forward Blocking Current | $I_{DRM}$ | **< 1 mA** | Leakage current in OFF state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Note:** During the experiment, the student is expected to identify and *read off* these parameters directly from the plotted V-I curve using the graph's gridlines and interpolation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Quadrants of the Static V-I Curve

The static V-I characteristic of an SCR is a **two-quadrant plot** divided into **four distinct operating regions**. Each region tells a different story about the device's physical state.

### Region 1: Forward Blocking Mode (OFF State) — Quadrant I, Initial Segment

- The anode is **positive** with respect to the cathode.
- The gate terminal is either open or has a sub-threshold signal.
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are forward-biased; junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased.
- Only a **small forward leakage current** ($I_{DRM}$, typically in microamperes to milliamperes) flows.
- As $V_{AK}$ increases, the leakage current increases very slowly (almost flat curve).
- This continues until $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$.

### Region 2: Forward Conduction Mode (ON State) — Quadrant I, High-Current Segment

- At $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$, the reverse-biased junction $J_2$ undergoes **avalanche breakdown**.
- The device switches abruptly from the high-voltage/low-current state to the low-voltage/high-current state.
- The voltage across the SCR collapses to $V_T$ (≈ 1.5 V).
- The anode current is now limited only by the **external load circuit**.
- Once turned ON, the gate loses control — the SCR will remain ON as long as $I_A \geq I_H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regenerative Internal Action:** The ON state is self-sustaining due to the two-transistor analogy of the SCR (PNP + NPN). Removing the gate signal does NOT turn the device OFF.

### Region 3: Reverse Blocking Mode — Quadrant III

- The anode is **negative** with respect to the cathode.
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased; junction $J_2$ is forward-biased.
- A **small reverse leakage current** ($I_{RRM}$, in microamperes) flows.
- The SCR behaves like a reverse-biased diode in this region.
- This mode continues up to the reverse breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$.

### Region 4: Reverse Breakdown Region — Quadrant III, After $V_{BR}$

- At $V_{AK} = -V_{BR}$, the device undergoes **Zener/avalanche breakdown**.
- The reverse current rises sharply.
- This region is **destructive** and **must be avoided** in practice.
- Fuses, snubbers, and voltage-clamping circuits are used to protect the SCR.

## 2.2 Gate Static Characteristic (V_G vs I_G Curve)

This is a **separate plot** showing the gate's input behavior.

- **X-axis:** Gate Voltage, $V_G$ (in Volts)
- **Y-axis:** Gate Current, $I_G$ (in mA)
- The curve is divided into three zones:
  1. **Non-trigger zone** — Below $V_{GD}$ (gate don't-trigger voltage), the SCR will NOT turn ON.
  2. **Uncertain / Transition zone** — Between $V_{GD}$ and $V_{GT}$, triggering is unreliable.
  3. **Reliable trigger zone** — Above $V_{GT}$ and to the right of $I_{GT}$, the SCR is guaranteed to turn ON.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are derived from fundamental semiconductor physics applied to the PNPN structure. Memorize these for the KTU 2024 lab viva and written exam.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BO} &= \text{Voltage at which } I_A \text{ rises sharply in forward blocking region} \\[4pt]
I_H &= \text{Anode current at which SCR turns OFF (read from V-I curve)} \\[4pt]
I_L &= \text{Minimum anode current required immediately after gate pulse to latch ON} \\[4pt]
I_L &\geq I_H \quad \text{(always; a fundamental SCR relationship)} \\[4pt]
V_T &= \text{On-state voltage drop} = V_{AK} \text{ at full load current} \\[4pt]
V_{BR} &= \text{Reverse breakdown voltage; rated as } V_{RRM} \\[4pt]
I_{DRM} &= \text{Maximum forward blocking (leakage) current at rated } V_{DRM} \\[4pt]
I_{RRM} &= \text{Maximum reverse blocking (leakage) current at rated } V_{RRM}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | How to Read from V-I Curve |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{BO}$ | Forward Breakover Voltage | V | Knee voltage where $I_A$ shoots up vertically |
| $I_H$ | Holding Current | mA | Anode current at which V-I curve "snaps back" |
| $V_T$ | On-state Voltage | V | Y-axis intercept of the ON-state segment |
| $V_{BR}$ | Reverse Breakdown Voltage | V | Knee on the negative $V_{AK}$ side |
| $I_{GT}$ | Gate Trigger Current | mA | From gate $V_G$–$I_G$ plot |
| $V_{GT}$ | Gate Trigger Voltage | V | From gate $V_G$–$I_G$ plot |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where are static characteristics used in real engineering?**
>
> 1. **Datasheet Verification:** Manufacturers plot these curves for every SCR. Engineers use them to select the right SCR for a given application.
> 2. **Protection Design:** Knowing $V_{BO}$ and $V_{BR}$ helps design snubber circuits and voltage clamping.
> 3. **Converter Design:** In rectifiers, choppers, and inverters, designers must ensure operating voltage stays below $V_{BO}$ in the OFF state.
> 4. **Latching Logic:** In digital triggering circuits, the gate pulse width is designed to ensure anode current crosses $I_L$ before the pulse ends.
> 5. **AC Phase Control:** Used in light dimmers, fan regulators, heater controls — the static curve determines the firing angle limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Transistor Model of SCR

The SCR is internally modeled as **two interconnected transistors**: a PNP transistor ($Q_1$) and an NPN transistor ($Q_2$). This model is essential to understand *why* the device latches.

### Step 1: Identify the Layers
The PNPN structure can be split as:
- $Q_1$ (PNP): Outer P layer → inner N layer → middle P layer
- $Q_2$ (NPN): Middle P layer → inner N layer → outer N layer

### Step 2: Write the Transistor Currents
Let $\alpha_1$ = common-base current gain of $Q_1$, $\alpha_2$ = common-base current gain of $Q_2$.

The collector current of $Q_1$ = $\alpha_1 I_A$
The collector current of $Q_2$ = $\alpha_2 I_K$

### Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inner P Layer (the Anode of $Q_2$)
The anode current $I_A$ entering the device splits into three parts:
- Current through $Q_1$ collector: $\alpha_1 I_A$
- Current through $Q_2$ collector: $\alpha_2 I_K$
- External gate current: $I_G$

### Step 4: Formulate the Anode Current Equation
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO} \\[4pt]
I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 I_K &= I_{CBO}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I_K = I_A + I_G$ (KCL at the cathode node):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A - \alpha_1 I_A - \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) &= I_{CBO} \\[4pt]
I_A (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2) &= I_{CBO} + \alpha_2 I_G
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $I_A$
$$
\boxed{\,I_A = \dfrac{I_{CBO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}\,}
$$

### Step 6: Latching Condition Analysis
The denominator $1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)$ determines the state:

- **When $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \ll 1$:** Denominator $\approx 1$, so $I_A$ is very small → **Forward Blocking state (OFF)**.
- **When $(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \to 1$:** Denominator $\to 0$, so $I_A \to \infty$ → **Breakover / Latching occurs**.
- **At breakover:** $V_{AK} = V_{BO}$, triggering the regenerative action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ is the criticality condition. Once met, the device turns ON and remains ON (because the high current keeps the alphas saturated at near-unity).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify Each Region on the Curve

| Region | $V_{AK}$ sign | $I_A$ range | Gate Signal | Visual Cue on Graph |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Blocking | Positive | $0 \leq I_A \leq I_{DRM}$ | None or below $V_{GD}$ | Flat, low curve near X-axis |
| Forward Conduction | Positive (small) | $I_L \leq I_A \leq I_{rated}$ | Already triggered | Vertical jump then flat low-voltage line |
| Reverse Blocking | Negative | $0 \leq \vert I_R \vert \leq I_{RRM}$ | None | Flat curve in Quadrant III |
| Reverse Breakdown | Negative | Sharp rise in $\vert I_R \vert$ | Irrelevant | Steep drop in negative current |

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship between $I_L$ and $I_H$

A common viva question in KTU 2024 labs:

> *"Why is latching current always greater than holding current?"*

### Logical Proof:
- **Latching current ($I_L$):** This is the current that must flow **through the device during the gate pulse** to ensure the regenerative action is fully established. The device has just been switched from a high-impedance blocking state to a low-impedance conduction state. The internal junctions need significant carrier injection to "lock in" the ON state. Hence, $I_L$ is **higher**.
- **Holding current ($I_H$):** This is the current at which the device **drops out of conduction** because the carrier injection can no longer sustain the latched state. The device is now losing carriers as current decreases. Hence, $I_H$ is **lower**.

Mathematically:
$$
I_L > I_H
$$

This relationship is verified experimentally by slowly decreasing the anode current from a high value (with gate triggered) and noting when the SCR snaps OFF → that current is $I_H$. Then triggering again and noting the minimum current at turn-on → that is $I_L$.

## 3.4 Lab Calculation Template (For Report Writing)

If during the experiment you record the following observations:

| Trial | $V_{AK}$ (V) | $I_A$ (mA) | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 0.05 | Forward Blocking |
| 2 | 50 | 0.10 | Forward Blocking |
| 3 | 100 | 0.20 | Forward Blocking |
| 4 | 200 | $V_{BO} = 200$ V, jump to ON | Breakover |
| 5 | 1.5 | 50 | ON state ($V_T = 1.5$ V) |
| 6 | 1.6 | 100 | ON state |

Then, from this data:
- $V_{BO} = 200$ V (read at the knee)
- $V_T = 1.5$ V (ON-state voltage)
- To find $I_H$: reduce $I_A$ until SCR turns OFF; suppose it turns OFF at $I_A = 8$ mA → $I_H = 8$ mA
- To find $I_L$: re-trigger and reduce $I_A$ immediately to find the minimum that latches; suppose $I_L = 15$ mA

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Skill Tested:** *Drawing the static V-I curve on a graph sheet, marking all four regions, and labeling the key parameters with units.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the SCR Experiment

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture of the SCR static characteristics measurement setup** — what each block does, and how the data flows from the device to the graph sheet.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Variable Voltage Source 0 to 30V] --> B[Voltmeter V1 across SCR]
    C[DC Gate Bias Supply 0 to 10V] --> D[Gate Resistor Rg 1k ohm]
    D --> E[Gate Terminal G of SCR]
    A --> F[Load Resistor RL 1k ohm]
    F --> G[Cathode Terminal K of SCR]
    G --> H[Ammeter A1 measures Anode Current IA]
    A --> I[SCR Under Test]
    I --> J[Anode Terminal A]
    H --> K[X-Y Plotter or Manual Graph]
    B --> K
    L[Gate VG vs IG Plotter] --> E
    E --> L
    M[Student Records Readings] --> N[Tabulate VAK and IA]
    N --> O[Plot Static VI Curve on Graph Sheet]
    O --> P[Mark VBO and I_H from Curve]
    P --> Q[Calculate Power Loss VT x IA]
```

## 4.2 ASCII Schematic: Experimental Circuit

```
   DC SUPPLY (+V)                         DC GATE SUPPLY (+Vg)
        |                                       |
        |                                       |
        +-------+-----------+                   |
        |       |           |                   |
        |     [A1]      [ SCR ]                 |
        |    Ammeter    A  K                   [Rg]
        |       |        |  |                   |
        |       |        |  +-------+-----------+
        |       |        |  |       |
        |       |        |  |     [V1]
        |       |        |  |    Voltmeter
        |       |        |  |       |
        |       +--------+--+-------+
        |                |          |
        |              [ RL ]     [G]
        |              1k ohm     Gate
        |                |          |
        +----------------+----------+
                         |
                       (GND / -V)
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Four-Region V-I Curve Annotation Map

This flow chart shows the **decision logic a student should follow** when reading the static V-I curve.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Read VAK and IA from meter] --> Q1{VAK Positive?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{IA near zero less than 1mA?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{VAK Negative?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Region: Forward Blocking Mode]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Region: Forward Conduction Mode]
    R2 --> Q4{IA greater than I_L?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R3[SCR is Latched ON]
    Q4 -->|No| R4[SCR will turn OFF when gate pulse ends]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{IA magnitude small less than 1mA?}
    Q3 -->|No| R5[Region: Forward Conduction with reverse bias - Invalid]
    Q5 -->|Yes| R6[Region: Reverse Blocking Mode]
    Q5 -->|No| R7[Region: Reverse Breakdown - Destructive]
    R1 --> P1[Mark VBO at the knee point]
    R6 --> P2[Mark VBR at the knee point]
    R3 --> P3[Mark I_H at the snapback point]
    P1 --> P4[Draw static VI curve with all four regions labeled]
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Layer Structure of SCR (PNPN)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Outer P Layer] --> J1[Junction J1 Forward Biased in ON state]
    J1 --> N1[Inner N Layer]
    N1 --> J2[Junction J2 Reverse Biased in OFF state - Key Junction]
    J2 --> P2[Inner P Layer]
    P2 --> J3[Junction J3 Forward Biased in ON state]
    J3 --> N2[Outer N Layer]
    P1 --> A[Anode Terminal A]
    P2 --> G[Gate Terminal G]
    N2 --> K[Cathode Terminal K]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Holding Current" of an SCR. How does it differ from Latching Current?**

**Model Answer:**

**Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the minimum value of anode current below which the SCR turns OFF (or drops out of conduction) and returns to the forward blocking state.

**Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the minimum value of anode current that must flow through the SCR during the gate trigger pulse to ensure that the device latches ON and remains conducting even after the gate signal is removed.

**Key Difference:** Latching current is the current required **at the moment of turn-on** to latch the regenerative action. Holding current is the current below which the device **drops out of conduction** during normal operation. Always, $I_L > I_H$.

| Parameter | Moment of Measurement | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $I_L$ (Latching) | At turn-on, during gate pulse | Higher (≈ 2× to 3× $I_H$) |
| $I_H$ (Holding) | During conduction, when current is reduced | Lower |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is meant by "Forward Breakover Voltage" of an SCR? What happens to the device when this voltage is exceeded?**

**Model Answer:**

**Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$):** It is the minimum forward voltage (anode positive with respect to cathode) across the SCR at which the device switches from the forward blocking (OFF) state to the forward conduction (ON) state **without any gate signal** applied.

**What happens when $V_{AK} > V_{BO}$:**
- The middle reverse-biased junction $J_2$ undergoes avalanche breakdown.
- The internal regenerative action (two-transistor model) is triggered.
- The device snaps from a high-voltage / low-current state to a low-voltage / high-current state.
- The voltage across the SCR drops to $V_T$ (≈ 1.5 V) and the current is limited by the external load.
- Once ON, the SCR stays ON until the anode current falls below $I_H$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Operating an SCR above $V_{BO}$ without a gate signal is a **violation of safe operating conditions**. In practical circuits, the working voltage must be kept well **below $V_{BO}$** — typically at 60% to 80% of $V_{BO}$ to provide a safety margin.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **static V-I characteristics of an SCR**. Clearly mark the four operating regions and label all key parameters. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An SCR has a forward breakover voltage of 400 V. If the maximum allowable RMS supply voltage is 230 V, calculate the safety factor. If the supply surges to 350 V peak, will the SCR turn ON without a gate signal? Justify. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

**Diagram:** (Refer to the ASCII schematic and Mermaid flow in SECTION 4)

**Procedure to obtain the static V-I curve:**
1. Connect the SCR in a DC circuit with a variable supply, ammeter in series, voltmeter across the SCR, and a load resistor.
2. Keep the gate terminal open (or connect a fixed gate bias if specified).
3. Slowly increase the DC supply voltage from zero.
4. Record the corresponding $V_{AK}$ and $I_A$ readings.
5. Plot $V_{AK}$ on the X-axis and $I_A$ on the Y-axis.

**The four operating regions:**

| Region | Location on Graph | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Blocking** | Q1, low $I_A$ | $V_{AK} > 0$, $I_A \approx 0$, $J_2$ reverse-biased |
| **Forward Conduction** | Q1, high $I_A$ | After $V_{BO}$, $V_{AK} = V_T$, $I_A$ rises with load |
| **Reverse Blocking** | Q3, low $\vert I_A \vert$ | $V_{AK} < 0$, $J_1$ and $J_3$ reverse-biased |
| **Reverse Breakdown** | Q3, high $\vert I_A \vert$ | At $V_{BR}$, avalanche, destructive |

**Parameters labeled on curve:** $V_{BO}$, $I_{DRM}$, $V_T$, $I_H$, $I_L$, $V_{BR}$, $I_{RRM}$.

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

**Given:**
- $V_{BO} = 400$ V
- $V_{supply, RMS} = 230$ V
- $V_{surge, peak} = 350$ V

**Step 1: Calculate the peak of normal supply voltage.**

$$
V_{peak} = V_{RMS} \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.22 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the safety factor.**

$$
\text{Safety Factor} = \dfrac{V_{BO}}{V_{peak}} = \dfrac{400}{325.22} = 1.23
$$

**Step 3: Check whether the SCR will turn ON during a 350 V peak surge.**

Since $V_{surge, peak} = 350$ V $< V_{BO} = 400$ V, **the SCR will NOT turn ON without a gate signal** during the surge.

**Justification:** For turn-on without a gate signal, the applied forward voltage must reach $V_{BO} = 400$ V. The peak surge of 350 V is below this threshold, so the middle junction $J_2$ will not undergo avalanche breakdown. The device remains in the forward blocking state.

**Final Answer:** Safety factor = **1.23**; SCR will **NOT** turn ON during the 350 V surge.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **two-transistor analogy of an SCR** and derive the expression for anode current $I_A$. What is the condition for the device to turn ON? (7 Marks)

**(b)** During an experiment on SCR static characteristics, the following readings are recorded. Plot the V-I curve, identify $V_{BO}$ and $V_T$, and comment on the operating regions. (7 Marks)

| S.No | $V_{AK}$ (V) | $I_A$ (mA) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 0.1 |
| 2 | 50 | 0.2 |
| 3 | 100 | 0.4 |
| 4 | 150 | 0.6 |
| 5 | 200 | 0.8 (knee point, sudden jump) |
| 6 | 1.5 | 20 |
| 7 | 1.6 | 40 |
| 8 | 1.7 | 60 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

**Two-Transistor Model:** The PNPN structure of the SCR can be split into:
- A **PNP transistor** ($Q_1$) with emitter at Anode (A), base at inner N, collector at inner P.
- An **NPN transistor** ($Q_2$) with emitter at Cathode (K), base at inner P, collector at inner N.
- The collector of $Q_1$ is tied to the base of $Q_2$, and the collector of $Q_2$ is tied to the base of $Q_1$ — forming a **regenerative feedback loop**.

**Derivation of $I_A$:**

Let $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ be the common-base current gains of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, and $I_{CBO}$ the leakage current.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_A &= \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 I_K + I_{CBO} \\[4pt]
\text{Since } I_K &= I_A + I_G: \\[4pt]
I_A &= \alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CBO} \\[4pt]
I_A (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2) &= I_{CBO} + \alpha_2 I_G
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_A = \dfrac{I_{CBO} + \alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}\,}
$$

**Turn-on Condition:**
- As $V_{AK}$ increases, $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ increase.
- When $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator $\to 0$, and $I_A \to \infty$.
- This is the **breakover condition**: the device snaps ON.
- Alternatively, applying a gate current $I_G > 0$ also drives the numerator up, achieving the same effect at a **lower** $V_{AK}$.

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

**Plot Description:** On a graph sheet, plot $V_{AK}$ (X-axis) and $I_A$ (Y-axis).
- For readings 1 to 5: a gently rising curve in forward blocking mode.
- At reading 5 ($V_{AK} = 200$ V): a **sharp vertical jump** indicating breakover.
- For readings 6 to 8: a low-voltage, high-current line in the ON state.

**Identified Parameters:**
- $V_{BO} = 200$ V (knee voltage at which the device snaps ON)
- $V_T \approx 1.5$ V (ON-state voltage drop, intercept on Y-axis of ON segment)

**Operating Regions Observed:**
- **Readings 1–5 (20 V to 200 V):** Forward blocking mode. $I_A$ is in sub-milliampere range — typical leakage behavior.
- **Reading 5 (knee):** Breakover point. $J_2$ avalanches.
- **Readings 6–8:** Forward conduction mode. $V_{AK}$ is low (≈ 1.5–1.7 V), $I_A$ is high (20–60 mA). The slope $dV_A / dI_A \approx 0.005$ Ω indicates a very low ON-state resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **breakover voltage** with the **breakdown voltage** — $V_{BO}$ is in the *forward* direction; $V_{BR}$ is in the *reverse* direction. Marks are deducted for mixing these up.
> 2. Many students forget to **label both axes with units and name** on the graph sheet. This costs 1 mark easily.
> 3. Failing to **identify the four regions** in the answer (not just draw them) loses 2 marks in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. The safety factor formula is sometimes wrongly written as $V_{peak} / V_{BO}$ — the correct form is $V_{BO} / V_{peak}$ (ratio must be greater than 1 for safety).
> 5. In derivations, students skip the step $I_K = I_A + I_G$ — this is a 1-mark deduction point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - **SCR = 4-layer (PNPN) device**, 3 terminals (A, K, G), unidirectional current flow.
> - **Static V-I curve has 4 regions:** Forward Blocking, Forward Conduction, Reverse Blocking, Reverse Breakdown.
> - **Forward Breakover Voltage ($V_{BO}$):** Turn-on voltage without gate signal.
> - **Reverse Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$):** Destructive avalanche in reverse bias — avoid!
> - **Holding Current ($I_H$):** Minimum current to keep SCR ON.
> - **Latching Current ($I_L$):** Minimum current to latch ON at turn-on.
> - **Always $I_L > I_H$** — a fundamental, must-know relationship.
> - **On-state voltage $V_T \approx 1.5$ V** — very low, hence low conduction loss.
> - **Two-transistor model:** $I_A = (I_{CBO} + \alpha_2 I_G) / (1 - \alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$.
> - **Turn-on condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$.
> - **Gate characteristic** has 3 zones: non-trigger, uncertain, reliable trigger.
> - **Lab skill:** Plot the curve, mark $V_{BO}$, $V_T$, $I_H$, and identify the four regions.
> - **Safety factor** = $V_{BO} / V_{peak}$ should be greater than 1 (typically 1.5 to 2 in practice).
> - **Gate signal does NOT turn the SCR OFF** — only reducing $I_A$ below $I_H$ does.
> - **Default OFF state:** With no gate signal and $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$, the SCR blocks forward voltage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Characteristics of MOSFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL307 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Static Characteristics of MOSFET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Static Characteristics of MOSFET

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is a four-terminal voltage-controlled unipolar semiconductor device in which the flow of majority charge carriers between the **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)** terminals is regulated by an electric field generated at a third terminal called the **Gate (G)**, which is electrically isolated from the conducting channel by a thin layer of **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)**. The fourth terminal is the **Body/Substrate (B)**. The *static characteristics* refer to the steady-state DC relationships between the terminal voltages and currents (namely, the **transfer characteristic** $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ and the **output characteristic** $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$) measured at low frequencies where capacitive (dynamic) effects are negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> In **PCMRL307 – Electrical Technology Lab**, Experiment 2 specifically requires the student to *plot the static (DC) drain characteristics and the transfer characteristics of a given MOSFET* and to extract the following key parameters from the plotted curves:
> 1. **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)** – in Volts
> 2. **Drain Resistance ($r_d$)** – in Ohms ($\Omega$)
> 3. **Transconductance ($g_m$)** – in Siemens (S) or millisiemens (mS)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **garden water hose** lying flat on the ground.

- The **water** flowing through the hose is analogous to the **drain current ($I_D$)** of the MOSFET.
- The **pressure tap at the far end** is the **Drain ($V_{DS}$)** that pushes the water.
- Now, suppose you place your **thumb over the hose opening** and gently press. The more you press, the less water flows. Your thumb is acting like the **Gate Voltage ($V_{GS}$)** of a MOSFET.
- The **minimum pressure you must apply with your thumb before the water flow noticeably reduces** is the **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)**.
- If you press too lightly (i.e., $V_{GS} < V_{th}$), the water flows almost unaffected — this is the **Cut-off Region**.
- If you press firmly and the hose is still partly open, water flows at a rate proportional to the pressure — this is the **Triode (Ohmic) Region**.
- If you press so hard that the hose is nearly pinched shut, the flow becomes constant (limited by the squashed opening) — this is the **Saturation Region**.

In short: **The Gate voltage "squeezes" the channel just like your thumb squeezes the hose — controlling the current electrically, without any physical contact.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Static" Characteristics?**
> The term *static* means the device is being studied under **DC (steady) conditions**, where the input and output voltages do not change with time. The curves we obtain show how the device *behaves at a fixed operating point* — analogous to taking a still photograph of a moving object. In contrast, *dynamic characteristics* deal with how the device responds to rapidly changing signals (switching speeds, capacitances, etc.).

### Symbolic Representation

The two common MOSFET symbols used in KTU lab manuals are:

| Type | Symbol Reference | Channel Type |
|---|---|---|
| **N-channel Enhancement MOSFET** | Arrow on substrate points **inward** (towards channel) | Majority carriers = **electrons** |
| **P-channel Enhancement MOSFET** | Arrow on substrate points **outward** (away from channel) | Majority carriers = **holes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants & Parameters used in MOSFET Analysis:**
> - **Electron mobility in silicon ($\mu_n$):** $\approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
> - **Hole mobility in silicon ($\mu_p$):** $\approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$
> - **Oxide capacitance per unit area ($C_{ox}$):** Depends on oxide thickness; typically $\approx 10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ F/cm²
> - **Permittivity of SiO₂ ($\varepsilon_{ox}$):** $\approx 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features of a MOSFET (Review)

A MOSFET is fabricated on a lightly doped **p-type substrate** (for an N-channel device). Two highly doped **n⁺ regions** are diffused into the substrate to form the **Source** and **Drain**. A thin insulating layer of **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** is grown over the region between source and drain, and a **metal (or polysilicon) gate electrode** is deposited on top of this oxide. The terminal connections are made via ohmic contacts to the source, drain, and substrate regions.

The thin SiO₂ layer is the **heart of the device** — it provides the extremely high input impedance (typically $10^{12}$ to $10^{15}$ $\Omega$) that makes MOSFETs ideal for the input stages of amplifiers, op-amps, and digital logic circuits.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. **Zero Gate Bias ($V_{GS} = 0$):** No conducting channel exists between source and drain. The device is essentially two back-to-back p-n junctions. Drain current $I_D \approx 0$ (the device is in **cut-off**).
2. **Small Positive Gate Bias ($V_{GS} > 0$, but $< V_{th}$):** A thin layer of negative charges (minority electrons) is induced at the silicon-oxide interface, but the channel is not yet fully formed. $I_D$ remains very small.
3. **Gate Bias reaches $V_{th}$:** The surface of the p-substrate undergoes **strong inversion** — a continuous n-type channel is formed between source and drain. The device is now **"turned ON"**.
4. **For a given $V_{GS} > V_{th}$:** As $V_{DS}$ is increased from zero:
   - The channel behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** (Triode region) for small $V_{DS}$.
   - The channel gets "pinched off" near the drain end at $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.
   - Beyond pinch-off, the current **saturates** to a value controlled by $V_{GS}$ alone (Saturation region).

## 2.3 The Three Operating Regions

| Region | Condition | Physical Meaning | Current Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cut-off** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | No channel formed | $I_D = 0$ |
| **Triode / Ohmic** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel is continuous, like a resistor | $I_D$ increases linearly then quadratically with $V_{DS}$ |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Channel is pinched off near the drain | $I_D$ is nearly constant, controlled by $V_{GS}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pinch-off does NOT stop the current (a common misconception):**
> When the channel pinches off near the drain, the electrons are *swept across the narrow depletion region* into the drain by the strong electric field. The current therefore does **not** become zero — it saturates to a value determined by the channel charge density, which in turn is set by $V_{GS}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you must memorize for the KTU University Exam and Lab Viva. These are HIGH-FREQUENCY questions — expect them in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Region of Validity | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Threshold condition | $V_{GS} = V_{th}$ | Boundary of cut-off & conduction | V (volts) |
| 2 | Drain current (Triode) | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | $0 \leq V_{DS} \leq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | A (amperes) |
| 3 | Drain current (Saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | A (amperes) |
| 4 | Conduction parameter | $k_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}$ | Constant for a given device | A/V² |
| 5 | Simplified $I_D$ (Saturation) | $I_D = k_n(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ | Saturation | A |
| 6 | Overdrive voltage (Effective voltage) | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Conduction region | V |
| 7 | Transconductance (parameter) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})$ | Saturation | S (siemens) or A/V |
| 8 | Transconductance (alternative form) | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS}-V_{th}} = 2\sqrt{k_n I_D}$ | Saturation | S |
| 9 | Output resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D}\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}=\text{const}}$ | Saturation | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Pinch-off voltage (drain side) | $V_{DS,\text{sat}} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Boundary of triode/saturation | V |
| 11 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Material/device constant | F/cm² |
| 12 | Body-effect factor (advanced) | $V_{th}' = V_{th} + \gamma(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert})$ | When $V_{SB} \neq 0$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of MOSFET Static Characteristics:**
> The plotted static characteristics are the **designer's map** of the device. From these curves, the circuit designer extracts parameters to:
> - **Bias the MOSFET** in the saturation region for linear amplifier design (e.g., in audio pre-amplifiers, op-amp input stages).
> - **Design switching circuits** (SMPS, inverters) by operating the device in cut-off (OFF) and triode (ON) regions.
> - **Predict power dissipation** $P_D = V_{DS} \times I_D$ for thermal management.
> - **Calculate small-signal parameters** ($g_m$, $r_d$) for amplifier gain calculations: $A_v = -g_m R_D$ (common-source amplifier).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drain Current Equation in the Triode Region

We begin with the fundamental **charge-sheet model** of the MOSFET. The inversion layer charge density (per unit area) at a position $y$ along the channel is given by:

$$
Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)]
$$

where $V(y)$ is the voltage at position $y$ along the channel (with $V(0)=0$ at the source and $V(L)=V_{DS}$ at the drain).

**Step 1: Write the current density**

The drift current at position $y$ is $I_D = -W \cdot Q_I(y) \cdot v_d$, where $v_d = -\mu_n E_y = \mu_n \dfrac{dV}{dy}$ is the electron drift velocity.

**Step 2: Substitute the expressions for $Q_I$ and $v_d$**

$$
I_D = W \cdot C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)] \cdot \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy}
$$

**Step 3: Separate variables ($V$ on one side, $y$ on the other)**

$$
I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)] \, dV
$$

**Step 4: Integrate from source ($y=0, V=0$) to drain ($y=L, V=V_{DS}$)**

$$
\int_{0}^{L} I_D \, dy = W \mu_n C_{ox} \int_{0}^{V_{DS}} [V_{GS} - V_{th} - V] \, dV
$$

The left side is simply $I_D \cdot L$. The right side evaluates to:

$$
W \mu_n C_{ox} \left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $I_D$**

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right] \quad \text{(Triode Region)}
$$

This is the **complete expression for drain current in the Triode (Ohmic) region**. The two terms represent the linear and quadratic contributions of $V_{DS}$ to the drain current.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Saturation Drain Current Equation

Saturation begins at the **pinch-off point**, where the channel charge at the drain end becomes zero:

$$
V_{DS,\text{sat}} = V_{GS} - V_{th}
$$

**Step 1: Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ into the Triode equation**

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 2: Simplify the bracket**

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \cdot \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Define the conduction parameter**

Let $k_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}$. Then:

$$
I_D = k_n (V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 \quad \text{(Saturation Region)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** In saturation, the drain current is **independent of $V_{DS}$** and depends **only on the square of the overdrive voltage** $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$. This square-law behavior is a hallmark of long-channel MOSFETs and is frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Transconductance $g_m$

The transconductance $g_m$ measures *how effectively the gate voltage controls the drain current*. It is defined as:

$$
g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS}=\text{constant}}
$$

**Step 1: Differentiate the saturation current equation with respect to $V_{GS}$**

$$
g_m = \frac{\partial}{\partial V_{GS}} \left[\frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2\right]
$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule**

$$
g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})
$$

**Step 3: Rewrite in alternative useful forms**

Since $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$, we can solve for $(V_{GS}-V_{th})$ and substitute:

$$
g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}}
$$

Or, eliminating $V_{GS}$ entirely:

$$
g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = 2 \sqrt{k_n I_D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of $g_m$:**
> A higher $g_m$ means a small change in $V_{GS}$ produces a large change in $I_D$. This is desirable in **amplifier design** because it yields a high **voltage gain** ($A_v = g_m R_D$ in a common-source amplifier).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:**
> An N-channel enhancement MOSFET has the following parameters:
> $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 10$, and $V_{th} = 1.5$ V.
> **(a)** Calculate the drain current when $V_{GS} = 3.5$ V and $V_{DS} = 4$ V.
> **(b)** Calculate the transconductance $g_m$ at this operating point.
> **(c)** Is the device in saturation? Justify.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** First, check the overdrive voltage:
$$
V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0 \text{ V}
$$

Check the saturation condition: $V_{DS,\text{sat}} = V_{OV} = 2.0$ V. Since $V_{DS} = 4$ V $> 2.0$ V, the device is **in saturation**.

Apply the saturation equation:
$$
I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \cdot (2.0)^2
$$

$$
I_D = 250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 1.0 \text{ mA}
$$

**Part (b):** Transconductance:
$$
g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{th}) = 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \cdot 2.0
$$

$$
g_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S} = 1.0 \text{ mS}
$$

**Part (c):** Yes, since $V_{DS} = 4$ V $> V_{DS,\text{sat}} = 2.0$ V, the device is in **saturation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick verification using the alternative formula:**
> $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.0 \text{ mA}}{2.0 \text{ V}} = 1.0 \text{ mS}$ ✓ (Matches!)

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section of an N-Channel Enhancement MOSFET

The following block-flow diagram illustrates the layered construction of a MOSFET, mapping the function of each physical layer and its corresponding terminal connection. This is the schematic representation most often required in KTU lab record submissions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[METAL OR POLYSILICON GATE LAYER] --> B[THIN SIO2 INSULATING LAYER]
    B --> C[P-TYPE SILICON SUBSTRATE BODY]
    C --> D[N+ SOURCE REGION]
    C --> E[N+ DRAIN REGION]
    A --> F[GATE TERMINAL G]
    D --> G[SOURCE TERMINAL S]
    E --> H[DRAIN TERMINAL D]
    C --> I[BODY TERMINAL B]
    B --> J[CHARGE INVERSION LAYER FORMS HERE WHEN VGS EXCEEDS VTH]
    F --> K[CONTROLS CHANNEL CONDUCTIVITY ELECTRICALLY]
    G --> L[HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE DUE TO SIO2]
```

## 4.2 MOSFET Circuit Symbol with Terminal Identification

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DRAIN D TOP] --> B[CHANNEL LINE]
    B --> C[SOURCE S BOTTOM]
    D[GATE G LEFT] --> E[VERTICAL LINE TO CHANNEL]
    F[BULK B RIGHT] --> G[ARROW POINTING INWARD FOR NCHANNEL]
    H[VGS APPLIED BETWEEN G AND S] --> I[CONTROLS ID]
    J[VDS APPLIED BETWEEN D AND S] --> K[PRODUCES DRAIN CURRENT]
```

## 4.3 Static Characteristics Curves — Block Architecture

The two key graphs that students must plot in the lab are: (1) the **Transfer Characteristic** $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ (at constant $V_{DS}$), and (2) the **Output Characteristic** $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$ (for various constant $V_{GS}$ values). The block-flow below shows the regions and parameters extracted from these curves.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STATIC CHARACTERISTICS OF MOSFET] --> B[TRANSFER CHARACTERISTIC ID VS VGS]
    A --> C[OUTPUT CHARACTERISTIC ID VS VDS]
    B --> D[REGION 1 CUTOFF VGS LESS THAN VTH]
    B --> E[REGION 2 SATURATION VGS GREATER THAN VTH]
    D --> F[ID REMAINS ZERO]
    E --> G[ID INCREASES QUADRATICALLY WITH VOV]
    C --> H[OHMIC TRIODE REGION VDS LESS THAN VOV]
    C --> I[SATURATION REGION VDS GREATER THAN VOV]
    H --> J[ID INCREASES ALMOST LINEARLY WITH VDS]
    I --> K[ID BECOMES CONSTANT PINCHED OFF]
    G --> L[EXTRACT VTH FROM X INTERCEPT OF SQRT ID VS VGS]
    K --> M[EXTRACT RD FROM SLOPE OF CURVE IN SATURATION]
    G --> N[EXTRACT GM FROM SLOPE OF ID VS VGS CURVE]
```

## 4.4 Typical Laboratory Circuit Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DC POWER SUPPLY VDD] --> B[DRAIN TERMINAL OF MOSFET]
    C[DC POWER SUPPLY VGG] --> D[GATE TERMINAL OF MOSFET]
    E[GROUND 0V] --> F[SOURCE TERMINAL]
    E --> G[BULK TERMINAL]
    H[AMMETER IN SERIES WITH DRAIN] --> I[MEASURES ID]
    J[VOLTMETER BETWEEN DRAIN AND SOURCE] --> K[MEASURES VDS]
    L[VOLTMETER BETWEEN GATE AND SOURCE] --> M[MEASURES VGS]
    N[VARY VGS IN STEPS] --> O[RECORD ID FOR EACH VDS]
    P[PLOT CURVES] --> Q[EXTRACT VTH GM AND RD]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Procedure Summary (Step-by-Step) — Memorize this for the Practical Exam:**
> 1. Identify the MOSFET terminals (D, G, S, B) using the datasheet pin diagram.
> 2. Connect the circuit as shown in section 4.4 above.
> 3. Fix $V_{DS}$ at a constant value (e.g., 5 V or 10 V) — sufficiently high to ensure saturation.
> 4. Vary $V_{GS}$ from 0 V in steps of 0.5 V, and record the corresponding $I_D$ values. This gives the **transfer characteristic**.
> 5. Now fix $V_{GS}$ at several values (e.g., 3 V, 4 V, 5 V, 6 V) and vary $V_{DS}$ from 0 to 10 V in steps of 1 V. Record $I_D$ for each combination. This gives the **output characteristic** family of curves.
> 6. Plot both graphs on a **graph sheet** with proper scale, labeling, and units.
> 7. From the transfer characteristic (or $\sqrt{I_D}$ vs. $V_{GS}$), extrapolate the X-intercept to find **$V_{th}$**.
> 8. From the output curves, find the slope in saturation to compute $r_d = \Delta V_{DS} / \Delta I_D$.
> 9. From the transfer curve, find the slope to compute $g_m = \Delta I_D / \Delta V_{GS}$.
