# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *threshold voltage ($V_{th}$)* of a MOSFET. What happens to the drain current when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The threshold voltage $V_{th}$ of a MOSFET is defined as the **minimum gate-to-source voltage** that must be applied to create a continuous conducting inversion layer (channel) between the source and the drain terminals of the device. It is the boundary between the cut-off and conduction regions of operation. Mathematically, $V_{th}$ is the value of $V_{GS}$ at which the surface of the semiconductor substrate just begins to undergo strong inversion, and the surface electron concentration equals the bulk hole concentration.

When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, **no conducting channel is formed** between source and drain. The MOSFET is in the **cut-off region**, and the drain current $I_D \approx 0$ (only a negligibly small leakage current of the order of nanoamperes or picoamperes flows). The device behaves essentially as an open switch.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between the *triode* and *saturation* regions of operation of an enhancement MOSFET.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis of Distinction | Triode Region | Saturation Region |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ |
| Channel shape | Continuous channel exists from S to D | Channel is **pinched off** near the drain |
| $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ | $I_D$ increases (initially linearly, then nonlinearly) with $V_{DS}$ | $I_D$ is **nearly constant**, independent of $V_{DS}$ |
| Device behavior | Acts as a **voltage-controlled resistor** | Acts as a **constant-current source** |
| Typical application | Analog switch, resistor in IC design | Linear amplifier (active load) |

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length University Exam Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**
> **Q(A).** **(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an N-channel enhancement-type MOSFET. Clearly identify the role of the SiO₂ layer and the depletion region. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ of an N-channel enhancement MOSFET in the **saturation region of operation**, starting from the basic charge-sheet model. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q(A)(a):

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The N-channel enhancement MOSFET is fabricated on a **p-type silicon substrate (body)**. Two heavily doped **n⁺ regions** are created on either side of the device surface, forming the **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)** terminals. A thin insulating layer of **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** — typically 50 to 100 angstroms thick — is grown thermally over the surface region between the source and drain. On top of this oxide, a metallic or polysilicon layer is deposited to form the **Gate (G)** electrode. Ohmic contacts are made to the source, drain, and body regions. The body terminal is usually connected to the most negative voltage in the circuit (for an N-channel device) to keep the source-body and drain-body p-n junctions reverse-biased at all times.

**Working Principle (3.5 marks):**
When $V_{GS} = 0$, no channel exists, and the device is OFF. When a small positive voltage is applied to the gate (with respect to the source), it creates an electric field that **repels holes** from the p-type surface and **attracts minority electrons** from the substrate and source/drain regions. This creates a **depletion region** beneath the oxide. As $V_{GS}$ increases, the electron concentration at the surface eventually exceeds the hole concentration — this is called **strong inversion**, and a thin **n-type inversion layer (the channel)** is formed connecting the source to the drain. The minimum $V_{GS}$ at which this occurs is the **threshold voltage $V_{th}$**.

Once the channel is formed, applying a positive $V_{DS}$ causes electrons to drift from source to drain, producing a **drain current $I_D$**. The **SiO₂ layer** plays two crucial roles: (i) it provides **electrical isolation** between the gate and the channel, giving the MOSFET an extremely high input impedance ($\sim 10^{12}$–$10^{15}$ $\Omega$), and (ii) the voltage drop across this oxide creates the electric field that controls the channel conductivity.

### Model Solution for Q(A)(b):

**Derivation of $I_D$ in Saturation Region:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The gradual channel approximation is valid (electric field along the channel is much smaller than the field perpendicular to it).
2. The drift current dominates; diffusion current is neglected.
3. The channel length modulation effect is neglected (i.e., the device behaves as an ideal square-law device).
4. The body and source are connected (i.e., $V_{SB} = 0$).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The induced inversion charge per unit area at position $y$ along the channel is:
$$
Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)]
$$

The drain current flowing through the channel of width $W$ is:
$$
I_D = -W Q_I(y) \mu_n \frac{dV}{dy}
$$

Substituting and integrating from source ($y=0, V=0$) to drain ($y=L, V=V_{DS}$):
$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right] \quad \text{... (1)}
$$

The boundary between triode and saturation occurs at **pinch-off**, when the channel charge at the drain end becomes zero:
$$
V_{DS,\text{sat}} = V_{GS} - V_{th}
$$

Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ into equation (1):
$$
I_{D,\text{sat}} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_{D,\text{sat}} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{th})^2
$$

Defining the conduction parameter $k_n = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}$:
$$
\boxed{I_D = k_n (V_{GS}-V_{th})^2}
$$

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**
> **Q(B).** **(a)** Sketch and explain the **static output characteristics** ($I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$) and the **static transfer characteristics** ($I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$) of an N-channel enhancement MOSFET. Clearly mark the triode, saturation, and cut-off regions on your sketches. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For an N-channel enhancement MOSFET, the conduction parameter is $k_n = 0.4$ mA/V² and the threshold voltage is $V_{th} = 1.0$ V. Calculate: (i) the drain current $I_D$, (ii) the transconductance $g_m$, and (iii) the output resistance $r_d$ (assuming a channel-length modulation parameter $\lambda = 0.02$ V⁻¹), when $V_{GS} = 3$ V and $V_{DS} = 5$ V. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q(B)(a):

**Output Characteristics Sketch (3.5 marks):**
The output characteristic is a family of curves of $I_D$ versus $V_{DS}$, each plotted for a fixed value of $V_{GS}$. For $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, $I_D = 0$ (no curve above the axis). For $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, each curve starts at the origin, rises almost linearly with $V_{DS}$ in the **triode region**, then "bends" and becomes nearly horizontal in the **saturation region**. The boundary between triode and saturation is the locus $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (a parabolic curve through the origin in the $V_{DS}$–$V_{GS}$ plane). In the ideal case, the saturation portion is a horizontal line, but in practice it has a slight positive slope due to **channel-length modulation** (modeled by the parameter $\lambda$).

**Transfer Characteristic Sketch (3.5 marks):**
The transfer characteristic plots $I_D$ (on the Y-axis) versus $V_{GS}$ (on the X-axis) at a fixed $V_{DS}$ chosen to be high enough to keep the device in saturation. The curve is **zero** for $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (cut-off region), and rises in a **parabolic (square-law) fashion** for $V_{GS} > V_{th}$. The X-intercept of the extrapolated curve is the threshold voltage $V_{th}$. A more accurate method of extracting $V_{th}$ experimentally is to plot $\sqrt{I_D}$ versus $V_{GS}$ — this gives a straight line whose X-intercept directly yields $V_{th}$.

### Model Solution for Q(B)(b):

**Given:** $k_n = 0.4$ mA/V² = $0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ A/V²; $V_{th} = 1.0$ V; $V_{GS} = 3$ V; $V_{DS} = 5$ V; $\lambda = 0.02$ V⁻¹.

**Step 1: Check the region of operation**

Overdrive voltage:
$$
V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3 - 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ V}
$$

Since $V_{DS} = 5$ V $> V_{OV} = 2.0$ V, the device is **in saturation**.

**Step 2: Calculate $I_D$ (with channel-length modulation)**

$$
I_D = k_n (V_{OV})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})
$$

$$
I_D = 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times (2.0)^2 \times (1 + 0.02 \times 5)
$$

$$
I_D = 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 1.1 = 1.76 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate transconductance $g_m$**

$$
g_m = 2 k_n V_{OV} = 2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.0 = 1.6 \text{ mS}
$$

(Equivalently, $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = 2 \times 1.76 \text{ mA} / 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.76$ mS, which accounts for the $\lambda$ factor — students should clarify which form they use.)

**Step 4: Calculate output resistance $r_d$**

$$
r_d = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 1.76 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 28.4 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the region of operation** before plugging numbers into a formula. A student who applies the saturation $I_D$ equation when the device is actually in the triode region will get the wrong answer and lose **2 to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring channel-length modulation** ($\lambda$ term). In ideal KTU problems, $\lambda$ may be assumed zero, but if it is given in the question, you **must** include the $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ factor — failing to do so is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency**: Writing $k_n$ in mA/V² and using $V_{OV}$ in V gives $I_D$ in mA directly. If you convert $k_n$ to A/V², you must also keep your final $I_D$ in amperes. Mixing units is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing $g_m$ and $r_d$**: Remember, $g_m$ is the slope of $I_D$ vs. $V_{GS}$ (input control), and $r_d$ is the inverse slope of $I_D$ vs. $V_{DS}$ (output resistance). Examiners *deliberately* test this distinction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall:**

- **MOSFET = Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor.** A **voltage-controlled**, **unipolar** device with **very high input impedance** (due to the SiO₂ insulating layer).
- **Threshold voltage $V_{th}$** is the **minimum $V_{GS}$** required to form the conducting channel. It is the **most important device parameter** and is extracted from the X-intercept of the $\sqrt{I_D}$ vs. $V_{GS}$ plot.
- **Three regions of operation:**
  - **Cut-off:** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ → $I_D = 0$
  - **Triode:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ → Device acts as a voltage-controlled resistor.
  - **Saturation:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ AND $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ → Device acts as a constant-current source.
- **Master formulas (memorize in this exact form):**
  - Triode: $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$
  - Saturation: $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 = k_n(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$
  - Transconductance: $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th}) = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS}-V_{th}} = 2\sqrt{k_n I_D}$
  - Output resistance: $r_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ (with channel-length modulation)
  - Overdrive voltage: $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$
- **Lab procedure (PCMRL307):** Fix $V_{DS}$ → vary $V_{GS}$ → record $I_D$ (transfer characteristic). Fix $V_{GS}$ → vary $V_{DS}$ → record $I_D$ (output characteristic). Always **verify the region of operation** before applying any formula.
- **Key relationship in saturation:** $I_D \propto V_{OV}^2$ (square-law) and $g_m \propto V_{OV}$ (linear).
- **For an N-channel device:** $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$, and $V_{th}$ are all **positive** (or zero). For a P-channel device, all voltages are **negative**.
- **Why MOSFETs are preferred over BJTs in ICs:** Higher input impedance, lower power consumption, easier to fabricate in large numbers on a single chip (no isolated p-n junctions required), and excellent scalability (Moore's law).
- **Common viva questions:** (1) Why is the input impedance of a MOSFET so high? (2) Why does pinch-off not turn the device OFF? (3) How is $V_{th}$ extracted experimentally? (4) What is the role of the body terminal? (5) Compare N-channel and P-channel MOSFETs in terms of mobility and speed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Waveform of Single Phase Bridge Rectifier Using SCR
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL307 - ELECTRICAL TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Output Waveform of Single Phase Bridge Rectifier Using SCR

# Module Experiment 2: Output Waveform of Single Phase Bridge Rectifier Using SCR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Single-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier** is a power electronic converter topology that employs four **Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs / Thyristors)** arranged in a full-bridge (Graetz bridge) configuration. Unlike an uncontrolled diode bridge, the DC output voltage magnitude is continuously regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** — the instant in each half-cycle at which the SCRs are triggered by gate pulses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The student is expected to *observe, sketch, and interpret the output voltage and current waveforms* of a 1-φ fully controlled bridge for **R-load** and **RL-load**, and to compute the **average (DC) output voltage** for a given firing angle. This experiment directly verifies the theoretical relation **V₀(avg) = (2·V_m / π) · cos α**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention used in KTU Labs:**
> - **T1, T2** → "Positive group" SCRs (fired together during the +ve half cycle of source)
> - **T3, T4** → "Negative group" SCRs (fired together during the –ve half cycle of source)
> - A pair (T1–T2) or (T3–T4) is termed a **commutation pair** because their simultaneous conduction transfers current from one pair to the other.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap with a Delay Valve" 🪢

Imagine two water pipes supplying a common overhead tank, one pipe carrying water during the "push" half of a pump's cycle and the other carrying it during the "pull" half. A normal tap (diode) opens the moment water pressure arrives. Now replace the tap with a **delayed-release valve** (SCR):

- Water pressure is present, but the tap **refuses to open** until you manually twist it at a chosen instant.
- The angle by which you delay the opening is the **firing angle α**.
- Delay it a lot → less water reaches the tank (low DC voltage).
- Open it immediately (α = 0°) → tank fills to the maximum (full uncontrolled rectifier output).

The SCR bridge does *exactly* this to AC electricity — it delays the start of conduction in each half-cycle by angle α, thereby controlling the DC level of the output.

---

### 1.3 Physical & Circuit Constants

- **Supply frequency (Indian standard):** **f = 50 Hz**  →  ω = **2πf = 314.159 rad/s**
- **One full cycle:** T = **20 ms** = **2π radians = 360°**
- **V_m:** Peak value of the AC supply voltage (V_m = √2 · V_rms)
- **Firing angle α range:** **0° ≤ α ≤ 180°** (practical limit ≈ 165° for commutation margin)
- **Conduction angle per SCR pair:** (π − α) for a resistive load; extends to (π − α + γ) for an RL load where γ is the extinction angle.

> [!TIP]
> 💡 A mnemonic to remember the firing sequence: **"T1-T2 fire first (Positive), T3-T4 fire next (Negative)"** — a clockwise traversal of the bridge. So the gate-pulse firing order is **T1 → T2 → T3 → T4**, each pair separated by 180°.

---

### 1.4 Relevance in Engineering Practice

Fully controlled SCR bridges are the **workhorse of DC motor drives (separately excited & series motors), DC battery chargers, electroplating rectifiers, HVDC transmission stages, and thyristor-controlled reactors (TCRs)**. Lab verification of their output waveform is, therefore, a foundational skill for any power-electronics or drives engineer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology

A 1-φ AC source (V_s = V_m sin ωt) feeds a bridge of four SCRs:

| SCR | Anode Connection | Cathode Connection | Fires At |
|------|------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **T1** | Source +ve terminal | Load +ve terminal | ωt = α |
| **T2** | Load −ve terminal | Source −ve terminal | ωt = α |
| **T3** | Source −ve terminal | Load +ve terminal | ωt = (π + α) |
| **T4** | Load −ve terminal | Source +ve terminal | ωt = (π + α) |

**Load types studied in this experiment:**
1. **Purely Resistive Load (R-load)** — SCRs turn OFF naturally at the zero-crossings of the source.
2. **Resistive-Inductive Load (R-L load)** — load current is **continuous**; SCRs turn OFF only when an opposite-polarity firing pulse commutates them.

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> Below, "ωt" denotes the angular time variable measured from the start of the **positive half-cycle** of V_s.

**Case A — R-Load (Discontinuous current mode guaranteed for α > 0°):**

- **Interval I — 0 ≤ ωt < α:**  
  No SCRs are conducting. V_s is positive but below the latching requirement; T1 & T2 are blocked.  
  ⇒ **V₀ = 0, I₀ = 0**

- **Interval II — α ≤ ωt ≤ π:**  
  Gate pulses are applied to **T1 & T2** simultaneously. They latch and conduct. The source appears directly across the load.  
  ⇒ **V₀ = V_s = V_m sin ωt, I₀ = V₀ / R**

- **Interval III — π ≤ ωt < (π + α):**  
  At ωt = π, V_s crosses zero and goes negative. Since load current cannot reverse through an SCR, T1 & T2 turn OFF (natural commutation). T3 & T4 are not yet fired.  
  ⇒ **V₀ = 0, I₀ = 0**

- **Interval IV — (π + α) ≤ ωt ≤ 2π:**  
  T3 & T4 are fired. Source voltage is negative, but the load sees a *positive* voltage because the bridge reverses the polarity at the load terminals.  
  ⇒ **V₀ = −V_s = V_m sin(ωt + π) = |V_s|, I₀ = V₀ / R**

- **At ωt = 2π:** Source returns to zero; T3 & T4 turn OFF. The cycle repeats.

**Case B — R-L Load (Continuous current mode likely):**

The inductive load **stores energy** and forces the load current to continue flowing **beyond the source-voltage zero-crossing**. Therefore:

- T1 & T2, once fired at ωt = α, continue to conduct past ωt = π.
- During π ≤ ωt ≤ (π + γ), V_s is negative but **V₀ remains positive** (still equal to V_s) because the SCRs are forced to conduct in the *reverse direction* of V_s — the inductor maintains current flow.
- The SCRs turn OFF only when T3 & T4 are fired at ωt = (π + α), reverse-biasing T1 & T2.
- **Extinction angle γ** is found from the transcendental equation:  
  **sin(α − γ) = sin α · e^(−γ / (ωτ))**, where τ = L/R.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🎯 **Key Observation for KTU Lab Viva:**  
> For an **R-load**, the conduction angle per pair is **(π − α)**. For an **R-L load**, conduction extends by an extra **γ** radians, giving a total of **(π − α + γ)** per pair. The *shape* of the output voltage waveform therefore differs visibly between the two loads.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and based on the standard 1-φ fully-controlled bridge with sinusoidal source V_s = V_m sin ωt.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Load | Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Average (DC) output voltage** | $V_{0,avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\,\cos\alpha$ | R-load & R-L load (continuous) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | **Average output current (R)** | $I_{0,avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi R}\,\cos\alpha$ | R-load | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | **RMS output voltage (R-load)** | $V_{0,rms} = V_m\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | R-load | Volts (V) |
| 4 | **RMS output current (R-load)** | $I_{0,rms} = \dfrac{V_{0,rms}}{R}$ | R-load | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | **Rectification efficiency (R-load)** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{0,avg}^{2}}{V_{0,rms}^{2}}$ | R-load | Dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | **Transformer Utilisation Factor (TUF)** | $TUF = \dfrac{V_{0,avg} \cdot I_{0,avg}}{V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s,rms}}$ | All | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Peak Inverse Voltage per SCR** | $PIV = V_m$ | All | Volts (V) |
| 8 | **Supply RMS current (R-load)** | $I_{s,rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | R-load | A |
| 9 | **Source power factor** | $\cos\phi_s = \dfrac{V_{0,avg}\, I_{0,avg}}{V_{s,rms}\, I_{s,rms}}$ | All | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Output frequency (fundamental)** | $f_0 = 2f$ (full-wave ripple) | All | Hz |

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Drive:** V₀(avg) controls armature voltage → controls motor speed (Ward-Leonard style modern replacement).
- **Battery Charger:** V₀(avg) set above battery EMF at start, then tapered by increasing α as battery charges.
- **HVDC Station Front-End:** Acts as the *rectifier* side of an HVDC link, feeding DC to the transmission line.
- **Thyristor-Controlled Reactor (TCR):** Same bridge, but used in reverse — α controls the effective reactance of an inductive shunt compensator in power systems.

> [!TIP]
> 🧠 **Examiner's Favourite Hook Question:**  
> *"Why is the formula V₀(avg) = (2V_m/π) cos α valid for both R and R-L loads?"*  
> **Answer:** Because the average is computed over the *full* cycle (2π). Although conduction extends differently in each case, the *integrated area under the output voltage curve* per cycle turns out to be identical, so the cosine relationship holds for both — provided the load current is **continuous** in the R-L case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive, line-by-line, and KTU-board-style**. Every algebraic transition is justified, and no step is skipped.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage (R-Load)

**Given:**  
- Source: $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$  
- Load: Pure resistance R  
- Conduction of pair (T1, T2): $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$  
- Conduction of pair (T3, T4): $\pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi$  
- In all other intervals, $v_0(t) = 0$

**Definition (Average Value):**

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 1 — Split the integral into the two conducting intervals:**

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\!\left[\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \;+\; \int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t + \pi)\, d(\omega t)\right]
$$

*(Logical note: in the second interval the source is negative, but the bridge reverses polarity, so the load sees +V_m·sin(ωt) for ωt in that range — but we use the standard form sin(ωt+π) = −sin(ωt), and the limits yield a positive contribution.)*

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \;=\; V_m \bigl[-\cos(\omega t)\bigr]_{\alpha}^{\pi} \;=\; V_m\bigl[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\bigr] \;=\; V_m\bigl[1 + \cos\alpha\bigr]
$$

*(Since cos π = −1, so −cos π = +1.)*

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second integral (by symmetry it is identical to the first):**

The output during interval IV is $v_0 = V_m \sin(\omega t + \pi)$ over $\omega t \in [\pi+\alpha,\, 2\pi]$. Let $\theta = \omega t + \pi$; then $d\theta = d(\omega t)$, and when $\omega t = \pi+\alpha \Rightarrow \theta = 2\pi+\alpha$; when $\omega t = 2\pi \Rightarrow \theta = 3\pi$. So:

$$
\int_{\pi+\alpha}^{2\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t + \pi)\, d(\omega t) \;=\; \int_{2\pi+\alpha}^{3\pi} V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta \;=\; V_m\bigl[-\cos\theta\bigr]_{2\pi+\alpha}^{3\pi}
$$

$$
= V_m\bigl[-\cos(3\pi) + \cos(2\pi+\alpha)\bigr] \;=\; V_m\bigl[-(-1) + \cos\alpha\bigr] \;=\; V_m\bigl[1 + \cos\alpha\bigr]
$$

*(cos 3π = −1, cos(2π+α) = cos α.)*

**Step 4 — Add the two integrals and divide by 2π:**

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 V_m (1 + \cos\alpha) \;=\; \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the trigonometric half-angle identity** $1 + \cos\alpha = 2\cos^2(\alpha/2)$:

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> ⚠️ **Careful!** The form $V_0 = (2V_m/\pi)\cos^2(\alpha/2)$ is *mathematically identical* to $(2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ **only when you also account for the sign convention used in the half-wave rectifier derivation**. For the **fully controlled bridge** with *symmetric firing*, the universally accepted KTU textbook form is:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{0,avg} \;=\; \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\,\cos\alpha\,}
$$

(The $\cos^2(\alpha/2)$ form appears in *half-wave* and *semi-converter* derivations — do not mix them up in the exam!)

**Numerical sanity check (KTU favourite):**  
Let V_m = 100 V, α = 60° ⇒ V₀(avg) = (200/π)(0.5) = **31.83 V** ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Output Voltage (R-Load)

**Definition (RMS):**

$$
V_{0,rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 1 — Using symmetry, compute over one conducting interval and double it:**

$$
V_{0,rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the identity** $\sin^{2} x = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}$:

$$
V_{0,rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t) \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[\omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the limits:**

$$
V_{0,rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[\left(\pi - \frac{\sin 2\pi}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)\right]
$$

Since $\sin 2\pi = 0$:

$$
V_{0,rms}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\!\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{0,rms} \;=\; V_m\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Extinction Angle γ for R-L Load

The SCRs T1 & T2 are fired at ωt = α and turn OFF only when T3 & T4 are fired at ωt = (π + α). Therefore the extinction angle γ is measured **from the source-voltage zero crossing (π)** to the actual turn-off instant, *relative to the firing of the next pair*. The extinction angle satisfies:

$$
\sin(\alpha - \gamma) \;=\; \sin\alpha \cdot e^{-\gamma/(\omega\tau)}, \quad \text{where } \tau = \frac{L}{R}
$$

> [!TIP]
> 🔬 This transcendental equation **cannot be solved analytically in closed form**; in KTU exams, it is *given* that γ exists, and the candidate must simply identify the relation. In lab, γ is **measured from the CRO waveform** as the angle from the source zero-crossing to the point where the output voltage *first* becomes zero after ωt = π.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Waveform Characteristics at a Glance

| α | V₀(avg) / V_m | Conduction per pair (R) | Output Waveform Description (R-load) |
|---|---------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **0°** | 0.6366 | π | Pure full-wave rectified sine (same as diode bridge) |
| **30°** | 0.5514 | 150° | Output "chopped" — gap of 30° at start of each half-cycle |
| **45°** | 0.4502 | 135° | Visible dead zones; V₀(avg) reduced by ~29% |
| **60°** | 0.3183 | 120° | Two-thirds of the available conduction has been "removed" |
| **90°** | 0.0000 | 90° | **V₀(avg) = 0**; theoretically no DC output |
| **> 90°** | Negative | 90°–(180°–α) | Inverter mode (V₀ reverses sign) — important for **regenerative braking** of DC motors |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** A 1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from a 230 V, 50 Hz AC source through a transformer. The load is purely resistive with R = 10 Ω. The firing angle is α = 45°. Calculate:  
> (a) Average output voltage  
> (b) RMS output voltage  
> (c) Average output current  
> (d) RMS load current  
> (e) Rectification efficiency  
> (f) PIV across each SCR

**Step (a) — V₀(avg):**

$$
V_m \;=\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 \;=\; 325.27\ \text{V}
$$

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 325.27}{\pi} \cos 45^{\circ} \;=\; 207.07 \cdot 0.7071 \;=\; \mathbf{146.4\ V}
$$

**Step (b) — V₀(rms):**

$$
V_{0,rms} \;=\; 325.27 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - 0.7854) + \frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{2}\right]} \;=\; 325.27 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}(2.3562 + 0.5)}
$$

$$
= 325.27 \sqrt{\frac{2.8562}{3.1416}} \;=\; 325.27 \sqrt{0.9091} \;=\; 325.27 \cdot 0.9535 \;=\; \mathbf{310.1\ V}
$$

**Step (c) — I₀(avg):**

$$
I_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} \;=\; \frac{146.4}{10} \;=\; \mathbf{14.64\ A}
$$

**Step (d) — I₀(rms):**

$$
I_{0,rms} \;=\; \frac{V_{0,rms}}{R} \;=\; \frac{310.1}{10} \;=\; \mathbf{31.01\ A}
$$

**Step (e) — Rectification Efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{V_{0,avg}^{2}}{V_{0,rms}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{(146.4)^{2}}{(310.1)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{21432.96}{96162.01} \;=\; 0.2229 \;=\; \mathbf{22.29\%}
$$

**Step (f) — PIV per SCR:**

$$
PIV \;=\; V_m \;=\; \mathbf{325.27\ V}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams depict the **circuit topology**, the **functional firing sequence**, and the **expected CRO waveforms** for a 1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier.

### 4.1 Circuit Topology — 1-φ Fully Controlled Bridge

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SOURCE[AC SOURCE 230V 50Hz]
        AC1((Vs +ve)):::srcNode
        AC2((Vs -ve)):::srcNode
    end

    subgraph POSGROUP[POSITIVE GROUP]
        T1([SCR T1]):::scrNode
        T2([SCR T2]):::scrNode
    end

    subgraph NEGGROUP[NEGATIVE GROUP]
        T3([SCR T3]):::scrNode
        T4([SCR T4]):::scrNode
    end

    subgraph LOAD[DC LOAD]
        LTOP((Load +)):::ldNode
        LBOT((Load -)):::ldNode
        R[R ohms]:::ldR
        LOPT[L Henrys]:::ldL
    end

    subgraph GATE[GATE PULSE TRAINER]
        GPT[alpha deg pulse generator]:::gptNode
    end

    AC1 --- T1
    T1 --- LTOP
    AC2 --- T2
    T2 --- LBOT

    AC2 --- T4
    T4 --- LTOP
    AC1 --- T3
    T3 --- LBOT

    LTOP --- R
    R --- LOPT
    LOPT --- LBOT

    GPT -. fires at alpha .-> T1
    GPT -. fires at alpha .-> T2
    GPT -. fires at pi + alpha .-> T3
    GPT -. fires at pi + alpha .-> T4

    classDef srcNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef scrNode fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ldNode fill:#b8e0d2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ldR fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ldL fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gptNode fill:#c4b5fd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Functional Firing Sequence & Conduction Intervals

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[0 deg: Vs starts +ve]:::neutral --> B{alpha deg reached?}:::neutral
    B -- NO --> A
    B -- YES --> C[T1 T2 FIRE: V0 equals Vs]:::pos
    C --> D[180 deg: Vs crosses 0]:::neutral
    D --> E{p + alpha reached?}:::neutral
    E -- NO --> F[T1 T2 still ON for RL load]:::rl
    E -- YES --> G[T3 T4 FIRE: V0 equals Vs again]:::pos
    G --> H[360 deg: cycle repeats]:::neutral
    F --> G

    classDef neutral fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef pos fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rl fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Expected CRO Output Waveforms (R-Load, α = 45°)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CH1[CH1 Vs Source Voltage]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Q1]**

**Q1. Define firing angle α of an SCR in the context of a 1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier. Why is α restricted to the range 0° to 180°?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **firing angle α** is the angular delay (measured from the instant the SCR becomes forward-biased) after which the gate pulse is applied to turn the SCR ON. In a 1-φ bridge supplied by V_s = V_m sin ωt, the SCRs T1 and T2 become forward-biased at ωt = 0, but conduction begins only at ωt = α.

**Range restriction:**  
- α = 0° means immediate conduction (output identical to a diode bridge).  
- α = 180° is the theoretical maximum because at that point V_s = 0 and the SCR can no longer be forward-biased.  
- Practically, α is limited to about **165°** to allow margin for commutation and to ensure the next SCR pair can be reliably triggered.  
**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for range justification.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q2]**

**Q2. State the expression for the average output voltage of a 1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier with a resistive load. Show that V₀(avg) becomes zero at α = 90°.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
V_{0,avg} \;=\; \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

When α = 90°, cos 90° = 0, therefore V₀(avg) = 0.  
**Physical interpretation:** At α = 90°, the conducting pair turns ON exactly when V_s starts decreasing from its peak, and the area under the positive V₀ lobe equals the area "skipped" at the start of the half-cycle, yielding a net zero DC average. For α > 90°, the average becomes *negative*, indicating the bridge operates as an **inverter** (regenerative mode).  
**(1 mark formula, 1 mark calculation, 1 mark physical meaning.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 2, End-Semester Pattern, Q13(a)/(b) — Dec 2024 Model]**

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier feeding a resistive load**. Sketch the waveforms of source voltage, gate pulses, output voltage, and output current for a firing angle of α = 45°. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **average and RMS output voltage** of the above converter. A 1-φ fully controlled bridge is supplied by a **230 V, 50 Hz** AC source and feeds a **R = 20 Ω** resistive load at firing angle **α = 60°**. Calculate (i) V₀(avg), (ii) V₀(rms), (iii) I₀(avg), (iv) PIV across each SCR, and (v) Rectification efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**1. Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**  
Draw the 1-φ bridge with four SCRs T1, T2, T3, T4 connected in bridge form across the AC source, with the load R connected between the +ve and –ve output terminals. Label the gate pulse generator α.

**2. Operation (3 marks):**  
- **0 ≤ ωt < α:** No SCR is fired. T1 and T2 are forward-biased but not yet triggered. Output V₀ = 0, I₀ = 0.  
- **α ≤ ωt ≤ π:** Gate pulses are simultaneously applied to T1 and T2. They conduct. Source appears directly across the load. V₀ = V_m sin ωt, I₀ = V_m sin ωt / R.  
- **π ≤ ωt < π + α:** At ωt = π, V_s reverses. For resistive load, current also reverses (cannot through SCR), so T1, T2 turn OFF naturally. T3, T4 are not yet fired. V₀ = 0, I₀ = 0.  
- **π + α ≤ ωt ≤ 2π:** T3, T4 are fired. V₀ = −V_s = V_m |sin ωt|, I₀ = V₀ / R.

**3. Waveforms (2 marks):**  
Provide the four waveforms (Vs, gate pulses, V₀, I₀) for α = 45° as shown in Section 4.3 of these notes. The V₀ waveform is a "chopped" full-wave rectified sine with zero portions of width α = 45° between successive lobes.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**1. Derivation of V₀(avg) (2 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.1 of these notes — reproduce concisely.

**2. Derivation of V₀(rms) (2 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.2 of these notes — reproduce concisely.

**3. Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**  
Given V_rms = 230 V, f = 50 Hz, R = 20 Ω, α = 60°.

**(i) V_m = √2 × 230 = 325.27 V**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 \cdot 325.27}{\pi} \cos 60^{\circ} = \frac{650.54}{3.1416} \cdot 0.5 = 207.07 \cdot 0.5 = \mathbf{103.53\ V}
$$

**(ii)**  
$$
V_{0,rms} = 325.27 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\!\left[(\pi - 1.047) + \frac{\sin 120^{\circ}}{2}\right]} = 325.27 \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}(2.094 + 0.4330)} = 325.27 \sqrt{0.8037} = \mathbf{291.86\ V}
$$

**(iii)**  
$$
I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} = \frac{103.53}{20} = \mathbf{5.18\ A}
$$

**(iv) PIV per SCR = V_m = 325.27 V**

**(v) Rectification efficiency:**  
$$
\eta = \frac{V_{0,avg}^{2}}{V_{0,rms}^{2}} = \frac{(103.53)^{2}}{(291.86)^{2}} = \frac{10718.5}{85183.3} = 0.1258 = \mathbf{12.58\%}
$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain how the **output waveform of a 1-φ fully controlled bridge rectifier changes when the load is changed from R to R-L**. Support your answer with neat waveform sketches and the concept of the **extinction angle γ**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1-φ fully controlled bridge feeds an **R-L load** with R = 5 Ω, L = 20 mH from a **220 V, 50 Hz** source at firing angle **α = 30°**. Determine (i) the average output voltage, (ii) the average output current, (iii) whether the load current is continuous, and (iv) the supply power factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**1. R-Load Waveform (2 marks):**  
For pure R, the load current and voltage are in phase. Both fall to zero at the source-voltage zero crossing. The output voltage V₀ consists of two discrete "lobes" per cycle, separated by zero gaps of width α.

**2. R-L Load Waveform (2 marks):**  
For R-L, the inductor **stores energy** during conduction and releases it after the source zero crossing. The load current therefore continues beyond ωt = π, *forcing* T1 and T2 to conduct even when V_s is negative. The output voltage V₀ remains **positive** (equal to V_s, which is now negative) for the extinction period π to (π + γ). The waveform shows **no zero gap** between the two positive lobes when γ ≥ α (continuous conduction).

**3. Extinction Angle γ (2 marks):**  
- γ is the angle (measured from the source zero crossing at ωt = π) during which the conducting SCR pair continues to conduct due to load inductance.  
- γ satisfies $\sin(\alpha - \gamma) = \sin\alpha \cdot e^{-\gamma/(\omega\tau)}$, where τ = L/R.  
- For large L (L → ∞), γ → α, and the output waveform becomes a continuous string of positive half-sine lobes — identical in *shape* to a diode bridge output, but with *reduced amplitude* controlled by α.

**4. Diagrammatic Comparison (1 mark):** Refer to the comparison diagram in Section 4.5.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** V_rms = 220 V, f = 50 Hz, R = 5 Ω, L = 20 mH, α = 30°.

**Step 1 — Compute basic quantities:**  
$$
V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 220 = 311.13\ \text{V}, \quad \omega = 2\pi f = 314.16\ \text{rad/s}, \quad \tau = L/R = 0.020/5 = 0.004\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Average output voltage (i):**  
$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 311.13}{\pi}\cos 30^{\circ} = 198.10 \cdot 0.8660 = \mathbf{171.56\ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Average output current (ii):**  
$$
I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} = \frac{171.56}{5} = \mathbf{34.31\ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Continuity test (iii):**  
The critical firing angle α_c above which the current just becomes continuous is found from:  
$$
\alpha_c = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R) = \tan^{-1}(314.16 \cdot 0.020 / 5) = \tan^{-1}(1.2566) = 51.49^{\circ}
$$

Since **α = 30° < α_c = 51.49°**, the load current is in the **discontinuous** region. ✗ Not continuous.

*(For continuous current, α must exceed α_c.)*

**Step 5 — Supply power factor (iv):**  
For an R-L load with continuous current assumption, the source current i_s is a square-wave of amplitude I₀ in phase with V₀, lagging V_s by angle (π − α)/2 in each half. A simpler KTU-acceptable approximation is:  
$$
\cos\phi_s \approx \cos\alpha = \cos 30^{\circ} = \mathbf{0.866\ \text{lagging}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to AVOID**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the Half-Wave vs Full-Bridge formula:** Many students mistakenly write $V_0 = (V_m/\pi)(1 + \cos\alpha)$ (half-wave) when asked about a *bridge* — the correct bridge formula is $V_0 = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$. **2 marks lost** in the viva/written exam.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the conduction extension in R-L load:** When sketching the R-L waveform, students often stop the V₀ lobe at ωt = π. This is **incorrect** — for R-L, conduction extends to ωt = π + γ, producing a visible "tail" or extra lobe beyond the source zero-crossing. Examiner will deduct **3 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Miscounting PIV:** The PIV across an SCR in a *fully controlled bridge* is V_m (NOT 2V_m as in a centre-tap or half-wave). Confusing the two costs **2 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Wrong rms formula sign inside the bracket:** Always remember $(\pi - \alpha)$ — never $(\alpha)$ or $(2\pi - \alpha)$ — in the RMS formula.
> 
> 5. **Drawing gate pulses on the wrong SCR:** T1 and T2 receive pulses during the **positive** half cycle, T3 and T4 during the **negative** half cycle. Reversing this gives full negative marks in the circuit-diagram sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before the lab exam**

- ✅ The fully controlled bridge uses **4 SCRs** arranged in a Wheatstone-bridge configuration; load is connected across the **DC diagonal**.
- ✅ Conduction sequence per cycle: **T1-T2 → T3-T4**, each pair conducting for **(π − α)** radians (R-load) or **(π − α + γ)** radians (R-L load).
- ✅ The **single most important formula**: $\,V_{0,avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha\,$ — valid for R-load and continuous-current R-L load alike.
- ✅ **PIV per SCR = V_m** (peak source voltage). This is half of what a centre-tapped rectifier requires — a key advantage of the bridge.
- ✅ Output frequency = **2f = 100 Hz** for a 1-φ bridge (full-wave operation).
- ✅ At **α = 90°**, V₀(avg) = 0; for **α > 90°**, the bridge operates in **inverter mode** (V₀ reverses sign — used for **regenerative braking** of DC motors).
- ✅ **R-load → discontinuous current** for any α > 0; **R-L load → continuous current** when α > α_c = tan⁻¹(ωL/R).
- ✅ Extinction angle γ for R-L load satisfies $\sin(\alpha - \gamma) = \sin\alpha \cdot e^{-\gamma/(\omega\tau)}$.
- ✅ The **gate pulse generator** must produce two pulses per cycle, 180° apart, with variable phase delay α (0° to 180°).
- ✅ In the lab, observe the **CRO waveform of V₀**: count the **two identical positive lobes per cycle** — this confirms full-wave rectification. The width of the **zero gap** between lobes equals α; measure it and verify $V_{0,avg} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$.
- ✅ The **load current waveform** is in phase with V₀ for R-load, but leads (or rather, lags in the negative direction) due to the inductor in R-L load.
- ✅ Always set the **oscilloscope in DC coupling** when viewing V₀ to capture the true average level.
- ✅ **Safety:** Ensure the firing-angle knob is at **α = 180° (minimum output)** before switching ON the supply; then gradually reduce α to the desired value. This prevents sudden inrush currents.
